3.2 Experience listing
Subject: THRT not fully engaged while pulling tubing hanger free.
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Pull Completion
Rep date: 03.Feb.2014
Keywords: TUBING HANGER
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: GE Vetco
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
RIH with THRT and engaged TH as per GE Vetco procedure. Set 2 MT weight on hanger, marked pipe. Rotated tool RH and
observed tool drop. Turned tool LH. Did not observe positive stop in J-slot profile. Suspected lock ring rotating. Applied 5 MT
overpull and confirmed tool engaged.
Pulled ring free with 40 MT overpull. Continued to pull, observed weight droping to string weight at 140 MT hookload. POOH and
inspected retrieval tool. No damage observed.
RIH and set 2 MT on TH. Observed mark at same spot. Rotated tool RH and observed tool drop. Rotated LH and did not observed
positive stop. Confimed tool holding with 140 MT overpull. Set down 4 MT weight on hanger and turned LH to force tool fully into
rotating lock ring. Repeated process. Picked up and pulled TH and completion free at 190 MT hookload. POOH and landed TH on
c-plate.
Tool was fully engaged when inspecting same on surface. Required several attempts to release THRT from TH. Had to rotate tool
using chain tongs, suspected allignment/hung-up on communication collar. Inspected TH and observed 1/3 lock ring J-slot was bent
upwards due to THRT not fully engaged during first pull.
Immediate solution:
POOH and inspected retrieval tool. No damage observed.
Future recommended solution:
THRT with hydraulic line should be used. Then the THRT can be locked in properly with use of hydraulics. This take some more
time, but is a safer solution.
Subject: 2 7/8" tubing equipment not fit for purpose
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG
Rep date: 13.Feb.2014
Keywords: TUBING
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: Statoil
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
Prior to run 2 7/8" tubing it was discovered that it had only been supplied manual slips.
2 7/8" tubing and handling equipment had been ordered by Statoil.
This resulted in a lot of time spent both on SJA/TBT/TOFS and also while tripping in hole.
While picking up the first joint various means of stabbing remotely was attemped with no success. Rigged up tong tailing arm and
function tested same. Continued running in with the stinger.
Immediate solution:
Performed a TOFS, SJA and TBT prior to use of manual slips.
Future recommended solution:
Non-standard tubular and related equipment should be specified by Statoil, but ordered by the rig contracor. That way we will get
the correct equipment.
Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 7 of 85

solution: Performed a TOFS, SJA and TBT prior to use of manual slips. Future recommended solution: Non-standard tubular and related equipment should be specified by Statoil, but ordered by the rig contracor. That way we will get the correct equipment. Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 7 of 85

Subject: 2 7/8" tubing equipment not fit for purpose
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG
Rep date: 13.Feb.2014
Keywords: TUBING
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: Statoil
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
Attempted to P/U first 2 7/8" joint from HTS with the air operated elevator. Unable to tilt and close center latch elevator, bail
rotator not designed for this type of elevator. Evaluated different options. Attempted
elevator using tugger. Unable to latch elevator.
Postponed the operation to a later stage.
2 7/8" tubing and handling equipment had been ordered by Statoil.
Immediate solution:
Decided to use the BX elevator, but discovered that correct inserts were missing. Had some problems with the TDS at the same
time, and decided together with town to go ahead and repair the TDS. Rigged down the Weatherford equipment.
Future recommended solution:
Non-standard tubular and related equipment should be specified by Statoil, but ordered by the rig contracor. That way we will get
the correct equipment.
Subject: Make up torque for 2 7/8" tubing
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG
Rep date: 14.Feb.2014
Keywords: CEMENT PLUG
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: Maersk Drilling
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
2 7/8" P110 8,7 # TPS TS-6 tubing was used as cement stinger. 2 7/8" drill pipe was not used due setup of Iron Roughneck.
The optimum make up torque for this tubing is 5339 Nm with maximum makeup of 5935 Nm.
A 300 m plug was to be set inside 7" liner and up inside 9 5/8" casing.
When conditioned mud prior to cement an RPM of 20 was used giving 8 kN. This is above the makeup torque for 2 7/8". Due to
this rotation was stopped.
When displacing cement and rotating pipe with 30 RPM 8 kN was seen. However it was necessary for a good cement job.
Immediate solution:
Future recommended solution:
Evaluate to use tubing/pipe with higher makeup torque.
Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 8 of 85

to this rotation was stopped. When displacing cement and rotating pipe with 30 RPM 8 kN was seen. However it was necessary for a good cement job. Immediate solution: Future recommended solution: Evaluate to use tubing/pipe with higher makeup torque. Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 8 of 85

Subject: No rotator sent out for Weatherford BX elevator
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG
Rep date: 16.Feb.2014
Keywords: CASING/LINER
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: Weatherford
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
No rotator was sent out with the Weatherford BX elevator for pulling the 10 3/4" x 9 5/8" casing and 13 3/8" casing. Weatherford
said that they did not have any available rotators to send out to the rig.
Immediate solution:
Used Maersk elevator
Future recommended solution:
Ensure that rotator is shipped out together with the casing handling equipment. Attempt getting one though a different vendor if
none available with casing contractor.
Subject: Lockdown assembly retrieval tool overfilled with hydraulic oil and air
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG
Rep date: 16.Feb.2014
Keywords: WELLHEAD/ASA
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: GE Vetco
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
The lockdown assembly/seal assembly retrieval tool (SRT) is set in retrieval position on surface by pushing up a sleeve on the outer
body of the tool past a ledge, locking the sleeve and tool in position for being able to engage and retrieve the lockdown assembly.
The SRT hydraulic oil chamber was overfilled with oil and air, causing the sleeve never to pass the ledge that locks the SRT in
retrieving position. As a result the sleeve slid back down again once the force that pushed the sleeve upwards was removed. It was
not noticed that the sleeve slipped back down again priro to running the SRT in hole for retrieving the lockdown assembly.
After the 1st mis-run the tool operator noticed hydraulic oil coming out of the chamber when pushing up the sleeve to set the tool in
retrieval position. The operator bleed of oil and air from the hydraulic oil chamber, but did not notice that the sleeve once again did
not pass the locking ledge, and slid back down again prior to running in hole.
When re-setting the tool in retrieving position after the 2nd mis-run, the operator noticed that the sleeve slid back down, and bleed
of more oil and air from the hydraulic chamber ¿ enabling the sleeve to be porperly locked in retrieving position.
Immediate solution:
Bleed of oil and air from the tool hydraulic chamber, allowing the tool to be properly set in retrieving position.
Future recommended solution:
Verify that the retrieval tool is in retrival mode priror to running the tool in hole.
Ensure that the tool has proper amount of hydraulic oil in chamber prior to shipping the tool offshore.
Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 9 of 85

in retrieving position. Future recommended solution: Verify that the retrieval tool is in retrival mode priror to running the tool in hole. Ensure that the tool has proper amount of hydraulic oil in chamber prior to shipping the tool offshore. Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 9 of 85

Well: NO 15/9-F-1 C
Subject: Oil leakage in seal assembly retrieval tool
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG
Rep date: 16.Feb.2014
Keywords: WELLHEAD/ASA
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: GE Vetco
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
After breaking out the seal assy retrieving tool (SRT) from the jet sub stand, the lock down assy was removed. When spinning the
SRT back into the jet sub stand the outer body continued to rotate after the make-up was done. This was caused by the weight of the
outer body and spinnig in to fast with the iron roughneck spinner.
As the outer body continued to rotate, the lower inner piston that keeps the SRT hydraulic oil in place was spunn out (left hand
threads), resulting in the hydraulic chamber being emptied.
Immediate solution:
Screwed the sleeve back into place and re-filled the tool with hydraulic oil. Used chain tongs instead of IRN for next ops.
Future recommended solution:
Ensure that caution is taken when spinning the SRT tool back into the jetting sub stand. Evaluate to use chain tongs instead of IRN
in future ops.
Subject: Excessive amount of slops created by not circulating over Hi-Vis pill prior to cement job
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG
Rep date: 23.Feb.2014
Keywords: CEMENT PLUG
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: Statoil
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
Excessive amount of slops was created when setting the KO plug on the F-1 B P&A.
The cement stinger was run in hole to the cut 10 3/4" casing, and the well circulated and the mud conditioned prior to setting a Hi-
Vis pill and settting the KO cement plug.
The Hi-Vis pill was set from the top of the cut 10 3/4" casing at 1457 m MD and up to the cut 13 3/8" casing at 1399 m MD (where
the cement KO plug would be set from). After spotting the Hi-Vis pill the stinger was pulled out to top of the cut 13 3/8" casing at
1399 m MD, and the KO cement plug set before pulling 80 m above theoretical top of cement and circulating out the spacer and Hi-
Vis pill.
The result from the above was that excessive slops was created from the Hi-Vis pill mixing with mud and spacer as the top of the
Hi-Vis pill had not been circulated out prior to setting the cement plug.
Immediate solution:
N/A
Future recommended solution:
It is recommedned for future KO plugs to spot the Hi-Vis pill, then pull out to the top of the pill with the stinger, and then circulate
out the top of the pill while conditioning the well for the cement job.
Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 10 of 85

is recommedned for future KO plugs to spot the Hi-Vis pill, then pull out to the top of the pill with the stinger, and then circulate out the top of the pill while conditioning the well for the cement job. Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 10 of 85

Subject: Excessive time spent on circulating hole clean and weighting up mud.
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 C, 17 1/2"
Rep date: 18.Mar.2014
Keywords: DRILLING FLUID
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: Baroid
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
Circulated 1 x BU, pumped a high-vis pill and circulated out of hole with little cutting over the shakers. Pumped a 2nd high-vis pill
and circulated out of hole before starting to weight up mud from 1.40 sg to 1.41 sg.
Immediate solution:
NA
Future recommended solution:
If little cuttings are seen over the shakers with the 1st hi-vis pill, then evaluate to wegiht up mud while circulating the 2nd hi-vis
pill.
Subject: Weight up mud from 1.40 sg to 1.41 sg while circulating 17 1/2" hole clean at TD
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 C, 17 1/2"
Rep date: 18.Mar.2014
Keywords: DRILLING FLUID
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: Baroid
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
Increased MW from 1.40 sg to 1.41 sg prior to POOH with 17 1/2" BHA to compensate for weight of cuttings in well to ensure
good hole conditions for running 13 3/8" casing.
The 13 3/8" casing was RIH without experiencing any drag. When running the 13 3/82 casing on well F-15 D, the casing had to be
washed down the last stand to get past a restriction.
Immediate solution:
NA
Future recommended solution:
Evaluate to increase MW while circulating hole clean to compensate for weight of cuttings in the well, to ensure good hole
conditions for running of casing.
Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 11 of 85

the last stand to get past a restriction. Subject: Increased ROP throguth out 17 1/2" section using Baker Hughes Kymera bit
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 C, 17 1/2"
Rep date: 27.Feb.2014
Keywords: REF BIT RECORD
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: Baker Hughes
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
Drilling the 17 1/2" section using a 17 1/2" Baker Hughes HP633F Kymera bit improved Net ROP compared to ROP in 17 1/2"
sections on the previous well in the Volve field were PDC bits were used.
Net ROP F-1 C 17 1/2" seksjon: Kymera bit - 29,3 m/hr. Bit grading: 0-1-WT-S-E-I-NO-TD
Net ROP F-15 D 17 1/2" seksjon: PDC bit - 16,9 m/hr. Bit grading: 1-2-WT-S-X-I-LT-TD
Net ROP F-1 17 1/2" seksjon: PDC bit - 18,1 m/hr. Bit grading: 4-3-CT-N-X-I-LT-TD
Net ROP F-11 T2 17 1/2" seksjon: PDC bit - 21,5 m/hr. Bit grading: 1-3-WR-G-X-I-BT-TD
Immediate solution:
N/A
Future recommended solution:
Same bit recommended for future 17 1/2" sections
Subject: Problem while making up the casing crossover (10 3/4" x 9 5/8")
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 C, 12 1/4"
Rep date: 07.Mar.2014
Keywords: CASING/LINER
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: Weatherford
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
After picking the 10 3/4" x 9 5/8" crossover; the crossover body was found too big to be grabbed and made up by Weather ford
tong. Some adjustment was done to fit the crossover in the tong but it leaded to have some compromise on make-up curve. The
torque curve showed the base and the optimum torque but curve continuity could not be verified due to the adjustment.
Immediate solution:
Changing the jaws and lifting the the Weatherford tong to grab the top 10 3/4" casing instead of crossover.
Future recommended solution:
It is suggested to have pre-made 10 3/4" and 9 5/8" pups to the Cross over.
Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 12 of 85

Immediate solution: Changing the jaws and lifting the the Weatherford tong to grab the top 10 3/4" casing instead of crossover. Future recommended solution: It is suggested to have pre-made 10 3/4" and 9 5/8" pups to the Cross over. Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 12 of 85

Well: NO 15/9-F-1 C
Subject: Leaking cement head swivel
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 C, Liner
Rep date: 19.Mar.2014
Keywords: CEMENTING
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: Weatherford Norge AS
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
when circulating the hole clean with the 7" liner on setting depth rotation was estbalished with 20 rpm.
It was observed that the swivel on the Weatherford cement head started leaking.
Immediate solution:
It was decided to stop rotation to prevent further damage to the swivel and maintain the option to rotate at a later stage.
During the cement job rotation was established and maintained while cement was displaced into the annulus.
Future recommended solution:
Improved quality of tools sent to the rig
Subject: Encountered tight hole when running 7" liner
Section: NO 15/9-F-1 C, Liner
Rep date: 25.Mar.2014
Keywords: CASING/LINER
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: Weatherford
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
Tight hole was encountered when RIH with the 7" liner that was managed to slide through with 10-15 MT WOB. Also experienced
pack-offs that had to be worked and reamed through with 20 rpm.
The above resulted in the liner being set a few meters shallow. The liner was RIH with a reamer shoe that had an OD of 8,25".
Immediate solution:
Reamed and worked though the restrictions and pack-offs.
Future recommended solution:
Evaluate to not run a reamer shoe with the liner as the OD of the reamer shoe is 8.25", while the OD of a regular guide shoe is 7.75
". It is thought that the large OD of the reamer shoe could be what is casusing the problems with tight hole. By running a regular
shoe with a smaller OD,one might be able to avoid any problems with tight hole.
Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 13 of 85

It is thought that the large OD of the reamer shoe could be what is casusing the problems with tight hole. By running a regular shoe with a smaller OD,one might be able to avoid any problems with tight hole. Classification: Open Status: Final Expiry date: Page 13 of 85

4 Activity highlights
4.1 NO 15/9-F-1 B, Preparations (25.Jan.2014 11:00)
START: 25.Jan.2014 11:00 3465 mMD
END : 31.Jan.2014 07:45 3465 mMD
OBJECTIVE
Kill the well and prepare the tubing for pulling.
SUMMARY
The well was handed over from production at 11:00 hrs 25.01.14.
Rigged up W/L BOP and riser. Removed xmas tree cap and installed short riser and W/L BOP. Schlumberger built and tested the
W/L toolstring for setting the EV0 plug. Filled the riser with 40% MEG and 60% water with the production MEG manifold.
Pressure tested the wireline rig up and made up BHA for running the EV0 plug. The EV0 plug was succesfully sat at 3147m MD.
Rigged up for W/L run # 2 (Tubing punch) and RIH and punched at 2942-2945m MD.
Pumped 70m3 of 1,03sg packer fluid followed by 10m3 of 1,50 sg CaBa brine pill followed by 42m3 of packer fluid, waited for 30
minutes to allowed the brine pill to settle into the 7" tubing/liner. Pumped 100m3 1,37sg OBM at 600-900 lpm until OBM in
returns. Circulated to condition mud, in total 4 x B/U.
M/U BHA for run # 3 (tubing cutter) and RIH. Positioned cutter at 2948,5m MD and performed cut.
Removed flowline from F-1 tree, drained xmas tree and removed same.
Nippled up BOP, installed diverter and R/D the equipment.
4.2 NO 15/9-F-1 B, Pull Completion (31.Jan.2014 07:45)
START: 31.Jan.2014 07:45 3147 mMD
END : 03.Feb.2014 20:30 3465 mMD
OBJECTIVE
Clear the borehole for tubing to make access for slot recovery
SUMMARY
P/U THRT and landed same in rotary. Installed bushings. M/U 5 1/2" DP stand to THRT. RIH w/ THRT on 5 1/2" DP stand from
surface to 21 m.
Engaged TH as per GE Vetco instructions. Pulled to 110 t (40t overpull), observed actuating ring release. Continued to pick up,
weight dropped of at 140 t hookload. POOH and inspected RT, no damage observed. RIH and engaged TH as per GE Vetco
instructions, no clear indication of tool beeing fully engaged. Suspected actuator ring rotating. Picked up to 140 t, set back weight to
3 t on TH. Re-engaged retrieving tool. Picked up to 190 t
hookload (120 t overpull) and pulled TH free. POOH w/ tubing and tubing hanger on THRT and 5 1/2" DP stand from 21 to surface
and landed TH in c-plate. Attempted to break out TDS, observed high torque on motor. Confirmed IBOPs not breaking out. Broke
out from stand, 20 - 30 kNm torque.
Released THRT from tubing hanger. Required several attempts to come free. Inspected hanger, observed first quarter of actuator
ring shoulder bent upwards. R/B stand and THRT in aft PRS. Removed communication collar and BPV from hanger. R/U 7" tubing
handling equipment and Weatherford power tong. Installed TH handling tool. P/U string and installed Weatherford compact spider
slips. POOH and L/O 7" tubing from 2926 m to 1860 m, removed cable clamps and spooled control line/cable. Continued to POOH
with 7" tubing.

and Weatherford power tong. Installed TH handling tool. P/U string and installed Weatherford compact spider slips. 4.3 NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG (03.Feb.2014 20:30)
START: 03.Feb.2014 20:30 3147 mMD
END : 19.Feb.2014 21:00 3148 mMD
SUMMARY
Attempted to P/U first 2 7/8" joint from HTS. Unable to tilt and close center latch elevator, bail rotator not designed for this type of
elevator. Evaluated different options. Attempted to tilt elevator using tugger. Unable to latch elevator.. Decided to go ahead with
TDS repair/change out of torque encoder.
RIH and sat asafelock plug at 619,9 m. Pulled casing hanger and 10 3/4" Tieback. Removed diverter.
Pulled overshot and removed BOP tensioners. Nippled down BOP. Disconnected surface riser and pulled same.
Rigged down cylinders, tensioner ring and centralizer.
Removed hatches and installed sheaves for guidewires. Ran guidewires and minireel. Disconnected production riser and pulled
same.
Had to wait due to weather window for running the HPDR.
Ran drilling riser joints, tensioning joint and BOP joint. Ran down with riser until just above guide funnel. Lowered connector
down inside guide funnel without any issues Landed drilling riser connector and sat down 20 T. Installed hot stab.
Pressured up by minireel to lock the connector. Performed OP test.
Installed hatches and build scaffolding. Tested riser and connector to 100 bar. Installed centralizers and tensioning system. Moved
BOP and installed diverter. Finalized nippling of BOP. Performed connector test to 232 bar.
P/U Safe Lock retrieving tool and RIH, stopped at 170 m and displaced riser to 1,37 OBM.
Continued RIH to above plug. Established circulation and latched onto plug. Released same. POOH and L/D plug and pulling tool.
P/U and M/U cement stinger with a muleshoe. RIH 350 m 2 7/8" tubing and continued on 5 1/2" DP. Washed down and tagged
EVO plug at 3148 m. Pulled up and conditioned mud.
Placed a 300 m cement plug. Pulled out of cement and circulated btm up before POOH. M/U and RIH with 8 1/2" Clean-out assy.
Washed down and drilled cement to confirmed hard cement at 2878 m. Confimed same by setting down 10 T.
Circulated btm up and POOH to 1457 m. Circulated to take down MW from 1,38 to 1,28 SG.
Continued POOH with 8 1/2" Clean-out assy.
M/U and RIH with a 9 5/8" cutting assembly to 1457 m MD. Cut the 9 5/8" casing with 800 lpm, and POOH and layed down
assembly. P/U a jet sub and a lockdown assembly retrieval tool and RIH to retrieve the 10 3/4" lockdown assembly. Experienced 2
mis-runs due to problems with the retrieval tool before retrieving the lockdown assembly on the 3rd attempt, with 20 ton overpull.
After retrieving the lockdown assembly, the extension sleeve was removed from the retrieval tool, and the tool RIH again to to
retrieve the 10 3/4" seal assembly. The assembly was retireved on the 1st attempt, with 12 ton overpull, with no trapped pressure
below. A 10 3/4" fishing spear was then P/U and RIH, engaging the 10 3/4" casing right below the hanger. The cut 10 3/4" x 9 5/8"
casing was pulled free without any overpull, and the well circulated to 1.40 sg OBM before pulling and retrieving the casing at
surface.
The 13 3/8" wearbushing was installed, and a 13 3/4" cutting BHA RIH and the 13 3/8" casing cut at 1399 m MD with 1000 lpm
and 80 rpm. The BHA was POOH before retrieving the 13 3/8" wearbushing and seal assembly, no trapped pressure below. A 13
3/8" spear assembly was P/U and RIH, engaging the 13 3/8" casing at 140 m, and the cut 13 3/8" casing pulled free and retrieved at
surface with no overpull.
The nominal bore protector was installed, and a 5 1/2" cement stinger RIH to top of the cut 10 3/4" casing at 1457 m MD. The well
was circulated and the mud conditioned for settitng a Hi-Vis bentonite pill and the KO cement plug. The Hi-Vis pill was spotted
from top of the cut 10 3/4" casing to the top of the cut 13 3/8" casing before pulling above and setting a balanced cement kick-off
plug to 50 m inside the 20" casing, at 1298 m MD.

8 1/2" Baker Hughes TD606X Talon bit (IADC code: M223) was selected for section. Bit drilled shoe track and 3m of new
formation
Parameters: 2200- 2400lpm,180 - 220 bar, 3m formation/ 32m cement, 7 drill hrs (5 hrs cement), 1circ hrs, 19 m/hr, 3 - 9 tons, 100
rpm, 12 - 15 kNm, ECD 1,50-1,53 sg. Due to problem with steering unit the BHA was pulled out.
Bit graded as 0 - 0 - NO - A X - I - NO - DTF and was re-ran for the second run.
Bit run # 2
Parameters: 2150- 2400lpm, 190 - 230 bar, 1035m, 44,9 drill hrs, 65,8circ hrs, 23,1 m/hr, 3 - 9 tons, 120-150 rpm, 12 - 22 kNm
ECD 1,49-1,52 sg.
Bit grading: 1 - 1 - WT - A - X - I - NO - TD
DRILLING FLUID
While drilling the cement at 9 5/8" shoe; the mud (Enviromul Yellow) was conditioned and weight up from 1.28 to 1,34.
At TD the hole was circulated clean with 3 x B/U.
CASING
Discribed in Liner section below.
MWD/BHA
A Hughes Christensen TD606X type bit, IADC M233 was selected by Baker for this section. The bit was part of the misrun BHA
and was re-run on run # 2. A Baker Hughes 6 3/4" AutoTrak steering unit was run to together with a Baker Hughes MWD and
LWD (logging while drilling) including radioactive sources for formation evaluation, directional control and vibration monitoring
purposes.
5 ½" heavy weight drill pipe (HWDP) and 5 ½" drill pipe were used above the MWD/LWD string.
CEMENTING
Pumped 15 m3 of 1,56 sg spacer w/700 l/min/ 83 - 58 bar. Isolated rig pumps from cement line, lined up and applied 30 bar against
IBOP with rig pumps. Lined up through cement hose and verified line-up. Dropped dart # 1 for bottom plug. Mixed and pumped
18,4 m3 1,90 sg cement slurry w/ 650 l/min. Pumped 890 l drill water after cement job. Rotated liner while pumping cement, but
had to stop rotation due to leaking swivel.
Released dart # 2 for top plug. Displaced cement with 900 l/min. Slowed down to 700 l/min prior to first dart releasing bottom WP
at 570 strokes, 61 bar SPP. Continued pumping with 900 l/min/ 35 - 96 bar. Reduced pump rate at 1350 strokes to 700 l/min/ 67
bar. Observed second dart releasing top WP. Continued displacement with 1000 l/min. Established liner rotation with 20 rpm prior
cement entering the annulus. Lowered rate to 800 l/min after experiencing loss tendencies. Slowed down to 500 l/min on the last 2
m3. Bumped the plug after 2430 strokes / 52,7 m3, 95 %pump efficiency, 120 bar. Increased pressure to 190 bar with rig pumps.
Equalized with cement unit and pressure tested the liner to 300 bar/ 10 min.

SUMMARY

1 Well objectives and results
1.1 Well objectives
The objective of the well is to produce oil from the Hugin Formation in the Northwest (NW) segment. The well
planning includes geosteering as a means to optimally place the well within the reservoir, aiming to connect
up the different fault blocks, maximise reservoir length and cover the full Hugin stratigraphy to allow for
flexibility in perforation strategy when the well is completed. In addition the geosteering will minimize the
length of any non-productive intervals, mitigating the risk of exit into either the Heather Formation above or
Sleipner Formation below, especially when drilling across faults where the throw is uncertain. The main
reference wells in the NW segment are pilot 15/9-F-11 A and failed water injector 15/9-F-1 B, which will help
to guide the geosteering. The well is planned to enter the Hugin Formation in an upflank position within the
NW segment, from where it will be geosteered as required, and will cross several prognosed faults as it is
drilled down flank within the NW with TD based on crossing the NW-SE oriented Volve Main Field boundary
fault and entering the Heather Formation.

1.3 Well results
The sidetrack 15/9-F-1 C reached TD on 14.03.2014 (after crossing the field boundary fault ‘F5’ (at 4004 m
MD RKB) at 4094 m MD / 3180.5 m TVD RKB in the Heather Formation. The well entered the Hugin reservoir
within the NW segment most likely crossing the prognosed fault ‘F1’ and found water-filled Hugin Formation in
this upflank NW section, contrary to pre-drill expectation.
The well continued to drill water-filled Hugin Formation in the upflank section before crossing the largest
prognosed fault ‘F3’ and entering the ‘F-11 A block’ which was oil-filled. The well did not exit the Hugin
Formation on crossing the fault ‘F3’ which was highlighted as one of the main pre-drill risks; instead drilled
sand-sand contact crossing from Lower Hugin in the upflank footwall, into Upper Hugin in the hanging wall of
fault ‘F3’. This was one of the main objectives of the pre-drill well planning, to ensure a section within Upper
Classification: Internal Status: Final Expiry date: 2014-12-30 Page 8 of 51

drilled sand-sand contact crossing from Lower Hugin in the upflank footwall, into Upper Hugin in the hanging wall of fault ‘F3’. This was one of the main objectives of the pre-drill well planning, to ensure a section within Upper Classification: Internal Status: Final Expiry date: 2014-12-30 Page 8 of 51

Hugin in the ‘F-11 A block’. The saturation change from water to oil across the fault ‘F3’, despite a sand-sand
juxtaposition, may be attributed to drainage effects in the upflank area which is communicating with existing
producers in the Volve Main Field, 15/9-F-14 and 15/9-F-12.
After achieving an Upper Hugin section in the ‘F-11 A block’ the well was steered down in relation to the
dipping Hugin Formation and drilled the full Hugin package from Upper Hugin then crossing the ‘offshore-
shale’ into the thief zone unit (zones 2.3; 2.2 and 2.1) and eventually into the Lower Hugin zones in a down
flank location in the ‘F-11 A block’.
After meeting the objective of drilling a Lower Hugin section in the ‘F-11 A block’ the well was geosteered up
relative to formation dip so as not to exit into Sleipner Formation below and in order to build and enter the
Hugin Formation in the ‘F-1 B block’ across fault ‘F4’. The well drilled from Lower Hugin in ‘F-11 A block’
across the fault ‘F4’ as prognosed, and into the Lower Hugin in the ‘F-1 B block’. The well-TD was set in the
Heather Formation, after crossing the Volve Main Field boundary fault ‘F5’ down flank to the northeast, which
came in later than prognosed and indicated a larger volume than prognosed in the ‘F-1 B block’; and the
additional reservoir length allowed for more perforation intervals in this block.
Overall, the total geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was 774 m MD long. Excluding water flooded
upflank section, a total of 500 m MD in oil-bearing productive Hugin Formation were encountered in the F-11
A and F-1 B blocks. Contrary to pre-drill expectations and modelling, no well exits into the overlying Heather
Formation were encountered during geosteering.
Initial perforations are placed in good to intermediate quality sandstones in the Upper Hugin and mid-thief-
zone (2.2) in ‘F-11 A block’ and in Lower Hugin in the ‘F-1 B block’. A total of 207 m were perforated in 13
runs (3 runs with 9 m and 10 runs with 18 m). Production was started Q2 2014 with an initial water-free rate of
~1500 Sm3/d; but this decreased to ~900 Sm3/d within a few weeks.

2 Geology & Geophysics
2.1 Geological setting and results
Reservoir section
The well entered the Hugin Formation within the NW segment most likely crossing the prognosed fault ‘F1’
and found water-filled Hugin reservoir in this upflank NW section, contrary to pre-drill expectation. The well
continued to drill water-filled Hugin Formation in the upflank section before crossing the largest prognosed
fault ‘F3’ and entering oil-filled Upper Hugin reservoir in the ‘F-11 A block’.
The well did not exit the Hugin Formation on crossing the fault ‘F3’ which was highlighted as one of the main
pre-drill risks; instead drilled a sandstone-sandstone contact crossing from Lower Hugin in the upflank
footwall, into Upper Hugin in the hanging wall of fault ‘F3’. The saturation change from water to oil across the
fault ‘F3’, despite a sandstone-sandstone juxtaposition, may be attributed to drainage effects in the upflank
area which is communicating with existing producers in the Volve Main Field, 15/9-F-14 and 15/9-F-12.
After achieving an Upper Hugin section in the ‘F-11 A block’ the well was steered down in relation to the NE
dipping Hugin Formation and drilled the full Hugin package, from Upper Hugin then crossing the ‘offshore-
shale’ into the thief zone unit (zones 2.3; 2.2 and 2.1) and eventually into the Lower Hugin zones in a down
flank location in the ‘F-11 A block’.
After meeting the objective of drilling a Lower Hugin section in the ‘F-11 A block’ the well was geosteered up
relative to formation dip so as not to exit into Sleipner Formation below and in order to build and enter the
Hugin Formation in the ‘F-1 B block’ across fault ‘F4’. The well drilled from Lower Hugin in ‘F-11 A block’
across the fault ‘F4’ as prognosed, and into the Lower Hugin in the ‘F-1 B block’. The well-TD was set in the
Heather Formation, after crossing the Volve Main Field boundary fault ‘F5’ down flank to the northeast, which
came in later than prognosed and indicated a larger volume in the ‘F-1 B block’; and the additional reservoir
length allowed for more perforation intervals in this block.
Table 2-1 provides the reservoir formation tops and zonation from Top Hugin Formation to well-TD. The
reservoir zonation is based on all available log information, mainly real-time image log interpretation from
azimuthal gamma ray (GR) and azimuthal density (by LithoTrak). Data are compared to a pre-drill Baker RNS
model and offset wells and re-matched in real-time during drilling in cooperation with PETEK G&G.
The zonation is somewhat uncertain in some sections; mainly upflank, as the well enters a thief zone (2.1)
across a fault; and the down flank resolution of the individual Lower Hugin sections; but overall the confidence
is high and the zonation assisted by correlation with the existing wells in the NW, 15/9-F-11 A and 15/9-F-1 B.
The main pre-drill risks and uncertainties in the NW included:
 Uncertainty on main faults, both lateral position along the planned well in m MD as well as throw
component

2.2 Seismic interpretation versus well results
In the 15/9-F-1 C oil producer Top Hugin Formation came in - 3 m TVD shallower than prognosed depth, well
inside the uncertainty on the depth estimate, which was ± 15 m TVD. Sonic logging or VSP was neither
planned nor performed in this high deviated well.
The well was expected to encounter 3-6 faults along the reservoir section. The drilled wellpath proved a total
of 7 faults, all with generally less fault throws than the prognosed faults. Faults F-3, F-4 and F-5 were
confirmed in the well. F5 was encountered 50 m MD after progosis. The other faults encountered were all
below seismic resolution. A seismic reservoir section with old and updated interpretation is illustrated in Figure
2-2.

17 ½” section (1364 – 2514 m MD)
The inclination ranges in this section by 9.5°-13.3°, 21.6° (start-end, max).
The sidetrack was initiated by time drilling from 1364 m MD. The well was then turned left from 300° to 230°
azimuth and into a 430 m tangent section at near 20° inclination. At 1481 m MD a survey was taken and a
planned wiper trip into the casing shoe was performed to check the hole condition. This along with subsequent
trips showed no indications in the shoe/sidetrack area. Several times the ROP was controlled to below
optimum due to cuttings handling and only two pumps online. Drilling the Grid sandstones demanded higher
bit weight and gave more torque in general. It was not possible to downlink while drilling with more than 5 t
weight on bit due to stalling the rotary, so the weight was picked off for downlinking on bottom. Drilling into the
Grid Formation showed a noticeable increase in over pull on connections. Drilling the Balder Formation gave
higher stick slip in general, but did not affect the ROP. A hard stringer on top of the Sele Formation was
observed. At 2360 m MD the well path continued with a right turn and a build started ending at TD with 13.3°
inclination and 26.6° azimuth.
12 ¼” section (2514-3056 m MD)
The inclination ranges in this section by 16.9°-57.6°, 57.6° (start-end, max).
Drillling was commenced from 2514 m steering with DLS of 2.6°/30 m build while drilling the Lista and the Ty
formations. In the harder Tor and Hod limestones an increased WOB to 20 t was required to achieve a ROP of
around 20-25 m/h. Also the build rate dropped dramatically and for most of the time 80 to 87% build force was
required to achieve the same build rate of 2.6°/30 m. However towards the end of the run, in the Hod
Formation, it was necessary to use 100% build force and hold back the ROP to 20 m/h, despite of that the well
path ended up 2 m below the plan at TD. Some erratic torque was observed while drilling the Hod Formation.
Attempted to run the Gyro, but due to no communication with the tool on surface this was cancelled.
Throughout the run the vibration was negligible. The CoPilot data was used to correct the surface WOB to the
downhole WOB. Both matched perfectly indicating good weight transfer to the bit. Average ROP for the run
was 23.5 m/h.
8 ½” section (3056-3059 m & 3059-4094 m)
The inclination ranges in this section by 61.0°-69.1°, 84.1° (start-end, max).
1st run: Drilled out cement and 3 m new formation. After FIT was performed, it was observed that the
communication with the AutoTrak was lost and decided to POOH.
2nd run: Initial build response was low. High build forces used to drill in the Hod, Hidra, Rødby formations to
achieve the required build rate of 3°/30 m without sacrificing the ROP. More or less continuous stick slip of
severity level 5 was observed down to 3164 m MD. Except for this, drilling dynamics for all mode stayed within
the expected window. The Draupne Formation was drilled with reduced flow rate due to hole stability
concerns. Continued operation in Heather Formation until 3229 m, where Hugin reservoir was entered after a
fault. Geosteered at ROP of ~29 m/h. Built inclination to 84° before starting to drop from 3376 m with DLS of
up to 3.4°/30 m. The Hugin formation was drilled with relative low weight. Section/well TD was set at 4094 m

2.3.2 Geosteering experiences
The pre-model from Baker Hughes’ RNS indicated high contrast towards top reservoir, i.e. Heather Formation
and very low contrasts internally, similar to previous recent geosteered wells, e.g. 15/9-F-15 D. Several faults
along the well path were prognosed, causing down-faulted reservoir segments. With uncertainty on the throw
component there was a risk of exit into Heather Formation above and Sleipner Formation below. For dip
calculations the near-bit azimuthal GR image data and azimuthal DEN image data (24 m behind bit) were
used.
Geosteering decisions were made by the PETEK and the RNS team. The RNS supervisor was present in the
Statoil office in Bergen during office hours, while RNS support outside office hours was covered by the
BEACON centre in Stavanger.
In total, 9 real-time geosteering decisions were performed. Changes in trajectory were done in order to
optimise wellbore positioning in the reservoir, both to maximise reservoir length whilst joining up potential
different fault blocks and to achieve specific well objectives such as targeting an Upper Hugin section after
crossing fault ‘F3’ and a Lower Hugin section down flank before crossing fault ‘F4’.
The near bit GR with 4 sector image helped significantly while geosteering, providing early information on
drilling relative up/down stratigraphy. Overall, the total geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was 774 m
MD long. Excluding water flooded upflank section, a total of 500 m MD in oil-bearing productive Hugin
reservoir were encountered in the ‘F-11 A’ and ‘F-1 B’ blocks. Contrary to pre-drill expectations, no well exits
into the overlying Heather Formation were encountered during geosteering, apart from the planned exit across
the boundary fault ‘F5’ down flank.
In Figure 2-5 the interpretation from Statoil PETEK is given, according to the recent valid geological
understanding. Differences to the interpretation from Baker Hughes’ RNS in Figure 2-6 can be remarked. Both
will be kept to provide the base for possible further investigations and reviews. PETEK’s interpretation today is
based on:
- In the regional extensional regime more normal than reverse faults are expected and can be
determinded. Also less faults can be assumed; to explain thickness variation not necessary structural
events rather sedimentological changes have trigged them.
- The properties of the distinct Hugin zone varies field-wide, thus to base the RES responses on just 1
reference well, can cause to strong restriction. Especially in Lower Hugin (zones 1.6-1.1) the internal
zonation can not be correlated field-wide with high certainty.

3.5 Pressure points
A total of 13 pressures measurements were taken along the well path, proving all 3 different blocks (Table
3-1). This was performed while POOH after TD on 16th of March 2014.
At the time of drilling 15/9-F-1 C producer both water injectors in the Volve Main Field (15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5)
were in operation, according to plan and maintaining the reservoir pressure at a level ~10-12 bar higher than
initial. In well 15/9-F-1 C the pressure points can be grouped into two main gradients, a shallow upflank
section with relative lower pressures corresponding well with Volve Main Field level and the other
corresponding to the downflank section with relatively higher pressures. The interpretation is that the upflank
area is in good communication with the Volve Main Field (+12 bar) and as a result, initial oil in this area had
already been drained by existing producers 15/9-F12 and F-14 by the time F-1 C was drilled. The increased
pressure deeper and further downflank can be attributed to earlier water injection in 15/9-F-1 B. The pressure
was much higher downflank after water injection in F-1 B but since this was stopped, the pressure is gradually
bleeding back to equilibrium with the Volve Main Field, but had not quite reached an equilibrium at the time of
the logging in F-1 C and was therefore higher (+15 bar) relative to the shallower section of the Northwest.

No sign of hydrocarbons have been reported while drilling the overburden. The Hugin reservoir, i.e. Upper

5 Pore pressures and hole stability
There were no indications that the prognosis were wrong. No observations of higher pore pressure or cavings
or hole instability have been reported.
5.1 Formation pressure above the reservoir
The prediction of the pore pressure gradient (PP) above the reservoir is based on the Eaton Sonic method.
- A hydrostatic pore pressure gradient is expected down to top Ty Formation. The pore pressure within
permeable Ty sandstones was measured in wells 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-15 S as 0.87 sg. This indicates
some 40 bar pressure depletion caused by production from the northern Sleipner Øst area. Recent
pressure data from Sleipner well 15/9-A-24, the well closest to the Volve Field, show stable pressure
since 2005, in line with the stop of production in the northern Sleipner Øst area. The pore pressure in
the Ty Formation within the Volve area is expected to be 0.87 sg, as measured in 2007.
- In the underlying upper part of the Shetland Group the pore pressure is assumed to increase gently up
to 1.05 sg within the Hod Formation. For the rest of the Shetland and the Cromer Knoll Group a rapid
increase in the pore pressure to 1.26 sg is prognosed.
- The maximum pore pressure within the Viking Group is expected to be 1.29 sg in the organic rich
Draupne shales at the top. The pore pressure within the underlying Heather Formation is expected to
be lower.
5.2 Pore pressure in the reservoir
The reservoir pressure in the Hugin Formation Volve Main Field is currently maintained at approximately 12
bar over initial pressure by injecting water downflank in wells 15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5. Throughout the field an
oil gradient of 0,0704 bar/m (datum depth 2855 m TVD, 320 bar) is estimated.
Pressure measurements were undertaken in all 3 penetrated blocks (Chapter 3.5). In general 2 pressure
levels have been differentiated. 12 bar overpressure compared to initial Volve Main Field oil gradient, thus in
good communication to the Volve Main Field, have been observed in the upflank block with partly water-
flooded Hugin reservoir and the oil-filled Hugin reservoir in the upper part of the ‘F-11 A’ block. However, 15
bar overpressure compared to initial Volve Main Field oil gradient encountered deeper, in the oil-filled Hugin
reservoir in the lower part of the ‘F-11 A’ block and the ‘F-1 B’ block, is because of the former short injection
test periods in well 15/9-F-1 B. The pressure was much higher and is gradually bleeding back to equilibrium
with the Volve Main Field. It was not quite there at the time of the logging.

5.3 Formation strength
The overburden gradient (OBG) is calculated from a density curve from well 15/9-F-12.
A minimum horizontal stress gradient has been calculated from a Breckels and van Eekelen relation based
on a method from Per Horsrud (2011). Initial reservoir pressure is used.
- Formation strength in the Balder Formation is reduced due to the presence of tuff layers. Nevertheless
XLOT data from well 15/9-F-14 showed a minimum horizontal stress gradient (ShG) of 1.68 sg in the
upper part of the Balder Formation. In the lower 32 m of the Balder Formation the formation strength is
reduced to 1.55 sg to account for local experiences.
- Within the Ty Formation the formation strength is assumed to be reduced due to depletion, but should
not cause any hole stability problems. It is well above the anticipated pore pressure in the overburden
section.
- The Minifrac data from Hugin Formation in well 15/9-19 A was revaluated in 2010. The result is a 19 bar
reduction in the minimum horizontal stress gradient for the Hugin reservoir and the formations below.
- The minimum horizontal stress gradient at initial pressure is been calculated.
The fracture gradient for the Volve area is set as 5% above the minimum horizontal strength, according to
the model.
- In the upper part of the Balder Formation an adjustment to only 1.73 sg was done to account for
reduced formation strength due to the presence of tuff layers.
A collapse gradient has been generated based on the method of Stassi d’Alia. Included in this method are
the initial pore pressure, the minimum horizontal stress, considerations of the UCS (unconfined compressive
strength), and the well path.
- The collapse curve in the Nordland and upper Hordaland Group shows a general increase towards the
hydrostatic pore pressure and surpasses it in the Skade Formation. The 36” & 26” sections were drilled
with 1.03 sg seawater/spud mud successfully.
- The Hordaland shales are normally associated with high smectite content and under-compaction,
causing instability and possibly higher pore pressure in the lower Hordaland Group. However, in the
Volve area the sandstones of the Grid Formations are generally present, acting as a pressure vent and
thereby preventing abnormal pore pressure. Nevertheless, shales within the Hordaland Group have not
been observed as unstable, when drilled at high inclinations and particularly when exposed to water
based drilling fluid.
o The UCS has been updated/increased while planning and executing the 2nd drilling campaign
and re-evaluating results from the 1st campaign. Affected intervals are in the lower Hordaland
Group and the lower Lista Formation.
- No incidents have been observed in the Cromer Knoll Group.
- The organic-rich Draupne Formation, and particularly the upper part, generally represents an interval
with risk for tight hole and collapse. No incidents have been observed when drilling with 1.32 sg
standard OBM. However, the highest inclination drilled was 71º.
- Only minor claystones have been observed in the sandstones of the Viking, Vestland and Hegre
groups. No incidents have been reported.

6.2 Input log data / composite curves
Almost all data was in memory. Exception is an interval of ~ 3 m (3755-3758 m MD RKB) where there are
gaps in the density log. The composite curves were created by Logtek from the memory data delivered by
Baker Hughes. The following composite curves are used for the petrophysical evaluation:
GR* : GRCFM from OnTrak
RHOB : BDCFM from LithoTrak
NPHI : NPCKLFM from LithoTrak
PEF : DPEFM from LithoTrak
RT : RPCEHM from OnTrak
DRHO : DRHFM from LithoTrak
CALI : CALCM from UltraSonic Caliper, part of LithoTrak
6.2.1 Corrections, editing
* Note that in the 8 ½” section a re-scaled NearBit GR (NBGRCFM) is spliced into GR for the last ~ 9 m.
NearBit GR has been re-scaled by -8 API in order to match GRCFM level.
Note in general that the final «average» density curve - RHOB (BDCFM) - from Baker is weighted with respect
to which of the 16 sectors / bins that provide the best density measurement (smallest standoff).
NPHI (NPCKLFM) is in limestone units, Caliper and salinity corrected and filtered.
Logtek’s RDEP (Resistivity Deep) curve = RPCELM, named RD in the OW Volve project.
Logtek’s RMED (Resistivity Medium) curve = RPCEHM, named RM in the OW Volve project.
Note that the RT curve used for saturation calculation, has been edited in intervals where RPCEHM is
affected by polarization horns or is saturated. This editing is kept on a specific curve in Geolog, named
RT_EDIT.
6.2.2 Depth shifts, splicing
No depth shifting has been applied, except in limited overlap intervals around kick off depth and hole sections
in Overburden. These depth shifts and the splicing of the individual runs are reported in Logtek’s info file;
WLC_COMPOSITE_1_INF_1.PDF which is stored in Petrobank. All depths are referenced to Driller’s depth.
Logtek’s general “philosophy” with respect to depth shifting in the transition zone between a mother well and a
kick off well is as follows: aim to keep the stop-coupled depths as shallow as possible, preferably at KOP. But
sometimes it has to be sat deeper (a few meters deeper, preferably in a shale if possible) due to a) if
overlapping curves are not on depth at KOP, or b) if the depth shifts have been so large, that the logs else will

6.3 Petrophysical evaluation method and input parameters
6.3.1 General
Petrophysical evaluation is performed according to the Volve petrophysical field model, described in report:
“Sleipner Øst and Volve Model 2006, Hugin and Skagerrak Formation, Petrophysical Evaluation”. November
2006. Author: Elin Solfjell, Karl Audun Lehne.
The petrophysical evaluation software used is Geolog, and the Geolog project is SLEIPNER_OST (at
Stavanger server: FROST_SVG).

VSH is used quantitatively in deriving permeability, and quantitatively indirectly in Netsand cutoff.
In order to pick GR and GR in a more objective way, and to avoid over-estimating of VSH, GR is
min max
“normalized” against VSHDN (VSH from DEN/NEU) by cross plotting GR versus VSHDN. VSHDN is
considered to be the most reliable shale indicator (except in intervals dominated by mica and heavy minerals).
This shale indicator compares porosity derived from the density log with porosity from the neutron log. If the
neutron log reads higher porosity than the density, is this believed to be due to the hydrogen content of
present clay minerals.

6.3.5 Permeability
The horizontal log permeability, KLOGH, is derived from the following equation based on multivariable
regression analysis between log porosity and shale volume (“normalised” against VSHDN) against overburden
corrected core permeability:
Heather Formation: KLOGH 10(28PHIF9VSH))
Hugin Formation: KLOGH 10(28PHIF9VSH))

6.3.7 Flag curves
Netsand flag
Cutoffs on PHIF and VSH are used to determine Netsand; SAND_FLAG = 1.
Hugin Fm.: SAND_FLAG = 1 when PHIF > 0.10 and VSH < 0.50.
These cut offs correspond to a overburden corrected core permeability of 0.5 mD, and are also applied for the
Heather Fm. In addition some manual editing might have been performed on the SAND_FLAG curve.

Summary of planned activities (24 Hours)

Date Well Complete: 2008-12-11
Summary of activities (24 Hours)
RIH with 20" casing from 1144 m to 1218 m. Had problems with 5½" elevators. Made up 18 3/4" WH. Ran in and landed same. Circulated 2 x casing volume 440 m³. Pumped 322 m³ 1.52 SG lead slurry at 1300 lpm, 25 - 28 bars pump
pressure. 20 m³ 1.92 SG tail slurry at 900 lpm, 25 bars pump pressure. Displaced cement with rig pump. Bumped plug at 9507 stks. 97.6 % pump efficiency.
Summary of planned activities (24 Hours)
Disconnect cement hose after pressure has been bled off. Release CART. LAY out cement head. Drop 2x Halliburton sponge balls and flush landing string. Pull out with CART and LAY out same. Prepare for skidding to F-12. Install BOP.

Final Well Report Doc. No.
Well NO 15/9-F-11, F-11 T2, F-11 A, F-11 B
Volve Field Valid from Rev. no.
1 Well objectives and result
1.1 Well objectives
Objectives of the 15/9-F-11 exploration pilot well were to prove oil in the Volve North prospect and to define the
field OWC. If the pilot would find an oil-down to situation (ODT), an optional openhole side-track further down
flank was planned. In addition the pilot was placed such that it could prove a shallower Hugin interpretation based
on the previous 2009 interpretation, which represented an additional prospect upside. An oil fluid sample was
planned to collect in any commercial find scenario.
Objectives of the second pilot 15/9-F-11 A were to prove oil and sufficient reservoir thickness in the Northwest
fault segment. If the pilot found an oil-water contact (OWC), a water sample would be taken to prove for initial
(perched water) or flooded conditions.
If the 15/9-F-11 North pilot well would encounter a sufficient oil column in Volve North segment, well 15/9-F-11 B
would be drilled to an optimal location further upflank and completed as an oil producer.
If 15/9-F-11 North pilot would find no or insufficient oil in the North prospect, 15/9-F-11 B would be sidetracked to
an infill location between existing producers 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-14 in the main field and downflank towards
existing injector 15/9-F-5. The aim was to geosteer the well in the upper part of the Hugin reservoir, which was
expected to be only partially drained.
Figure 1-1 shows the pre-drill Top Hugin map and the planned well paths.

1.3 Well results
1.3.1 15/9-F-11 / 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect
The well reached on May 4th 2013 at 4561,8 m MD / 3345,2 m MSL its total depth (TD) in the Sleipner Formation.
It encountered water-filled Hugin with some residual oil on logs and cuttings. Top Hugin Formation was
encountered 27 m TVD deeper than expected at 3181 m MSL. Full Hugin stratigraphy was present (104 m TVD /
~98 m TVT thickness). Reservoir properties were slightly poorer than expected, compared to offset wells at similar
depth. Pressure measurements were showing a reservoir pressure 13-14 bar over initial, relative to 15/9-19 BT2
and 15/9-F-15 A water gradient, but in accordance with the main field pressure, which was ~12 bar over initial at
the time. No hydrocarbon indications in Upper Jurassic were observed.
The well proved a dry Volve North prospect. Pre-drill prospect evaluation assumed a P of 34% with main risk
g
elements trap geometry (55%) due to poor seismic control and source migration (70%) given a possible charge
through the Upper Jurassic. The prospect model included a potentially deep OWC contact (down to 3200 m MSL
with no OWC drilled on Volve).
Post-drill well results support a migration fairway through the Hugin Formation into the main field. The prospect
trap however failed due to depth and geometry. Pressure measurements indicate that the aquifer communicates
with the main field. Top Hugin Formation at 3181 m MSL can be considered a water-up to (WUT) observation for
the main field.
A dry North prospect resulted in the cancellation of an immediate 15/9-F-11 B North producer and subsequent
15/9-F-1 A injector option.
1.3.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment
The planned sidetrack reached TD on May 21st 2013 at 3762,0 m MD / 3071,6 m MSL in the Sleipner Formation.
It encountered oil-filled Hugin Formation at 2944 m MSL, thus 25 m TVD deeper than expected. Full Hugin
stratigraphy was present (82 m TVD / ~64 m TVT thickness). Reservoir properties were good, but generally higher
water saturations than in main field oil-bearing wells were observed. Initial pressure measurements were ~12 bar
over initial and in accordance with main field pressure at the time. This indicates pressure communication to the
main field. Time lapse pressure points were taken 48 h later, after injection in main field was closed. This led to 6
bar pressure drop in the main field well 15/9-F-15 C. In the 15/9-F-11 A pilot a ~0.2-0.9 bar pressure drop was
observed, which was consistent with prognosis from the simulation model.
The well proved a thick upside oil column in the NW segment, which is in pressure communication with the main
field. In-place oil volumes in the segment are estimated with ~1.4 MSm3.

1.3.3 15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field
The planned sidetrack reached TD on June 6th 2013 at 4770,3 m MD / 3203 m MSL in the Sleipner Formation. It
encountered an oil-filled Upper Hugin (zone 3) in heel and toe with good reservoir qualities similar to offset wells.
The total geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long. A total of ~760 m MD in gross oil-
bearing productive Upper Hugin were encountered. Some non-productive Heather Formation and Hugin Thief
Zone (zone 2) intervals were drilled, mainly due to predominantly sub-seismic faulting. Towards the toe, the well
was dropped into water-flooded Thief Zone and Lower Hugin (zone 1) due to steering tool failure. TD was set in
the Sleipner Formation. An exit of the field through the main fault, as expected, was not observed.
With high oil saturation in both toe and heel, the well encountered upside volumes in the targeted Upper Hugin
zone. Expectation was that the heel would be water-filled as indicated by reservoir simulation and 4D seismic,
while mainly the downflank toe section would encounter undrained oil. At ~4700 m MD / 3147 m MSL a marked
drop in resistivity in the Lower Hugin may indicate that the well penetrated the initial field OWC. Water-filled zones
just above showed resistivities, which are consistent with injected Utsira water (+/ produced water).
Initial perforations were placed in good to intermediate quality Upper Hugin sandstones in the toe of well with
optional later perforation in the heel. A total of 93 m were perforated in 8 runs. Production was started July 24th
2013 with an initial nearly water-free rate of ~1000 Sm3/d. Drawdown is limited to avoid early water breakthrough.
Figure 2-1 shows the post-drill Top Hugin Formation depth map with the actual well paths.

2 Geology & Geophysics
2.1 Geological setting and results
2.1.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect
Overburden
Figure 2-1 shows the stratigraphy in the pilot from the Hod Formation down to TD in the Sleipner Formation. Prior
to entering the the Hugin reservoir the well encountered a thick package of Upper Jurassic Draupne and Heather
formations as expected. No significant Intra Draupne sandstones were observed. Thin sandy stringers are mostly
cemented. No indications for hydrocarbons on cuttings or logs were observed.
Figure 2-1 also shows image log interpretation on memory data as delivered by Baker Hughes (reservoir
navigation services, RNS) on memory data. Note that tadpoles are shown undifferentiated/unclassified for type or
confidence level and hence have a high ‘noise level’ (due to picks of cemented intervals etc.). Overall, they are
consistent with the expected structural grain. The well path is transported out in the Shetland Group and dropped
into the downfaulted North prospect while crossing the major fault separating the prospect from the main field.
Tadpoles down to the Draupne Formation indicate a generally North dipping structure with angles of ~10-20°. At
the Heather Formation level structural dip jumps to ~50°, then decreasing with depth into the Hugin Formation to
~30°. This indicates a structural drag along the large fault to the South. Formation is dipping generally to the NNE
to NE which is approximately consistent with the formation dip on the Top Hugin Formation depth map.
Reservoir section
A complete Hugin stratigraphy was encountered in the well. Lithostratigraphic subzonation can easily be
correlated with offset wells indicating no significant lateral facies changes.

Oil shows in the well are documented in Chapter 4. Residual oil intervals on logs are documented in Chapter 6.
The presence of some hydrocarbons in the Hugin Formation supports a migration through the Hugin Formation
from the kitchen in the North into the main field via the 15/9-F-11 T2 location. An additional fairway in Intra
Draupne sandstones has not been proven.

The thickness of the Hugin Formation was with 82 m TVD / ~64 m TVT near twice the expected. No direct
indications for faults on RT image logs are observed (Figure 2-3; image log tadpoles track 11). Note that
downfaulting would lead to an apparent thickening of the Hugin unit.
The reservoir section is overall very sandy as indicated by a N/G of 86%. Average porosity of 20% is comparable
to offset wells at similar depth. Reference to Chapter 6 for full petrophysical evaluation and CPI. Main differences
to offset wells in the main field are the higher water saturations (Sw) observed consistently throughout the entire
Hugin section. Average Sw is 32%, and hence >10% higher than in main field. Based on the fact that these high
saturations are observed throughout the Hugin reservoir and not bound to individual zones, they are not
considered drainage effects, and overall difficult to explain.
Pre-drill vs post-drill segment evaluation
Prior to drilling, the segment was risked with a P of 63%. The main risk elements were reservoir presence (70%)
g
due to potential erosion and source migration (90%) given a charge through Hugin via the main field and a
possible disconnect due to faulting.
The well proved a thick upside oil column in the NW segment. Pressure points show full communication with the
main field (Chapter 3.5.2). In-place oil volumes in the segment are estimated with ~1.4 MSm3. The main
uncertainties for in-place volumes are thickness of Hugin further upflank from the 15/9-F-11 A location and the
location of the major segment bounding faults especially also towards the salt collapse area in the South.

2.1.3 15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field
Reservoir section
Figure 2-4 shows the reservoir section with the reservoir zonation in track 4. Table 2-1 provides the zonation as
list. Geosteering experiences are summarised in Chapter 2.3.4. Reservoir zonation is based on the curtain model
provided by Baker Hughes’ realtime reservoir navigation service (RNS) using all available log information,
specifically azimuthal resistivity readings (by AziTrak) and real-time image log interpretation from azimuthal
gamma ray (GR) and azimuthal density (by LithoTrak). Data is compared to a pre-drill model and offset wells
(mainly well 15/9-F-5), and re-matched in real-time during drilling in cooperation with PETEK G&G.
Zonation documented in this report deviates from the final Baker Hughes delivery in some small intervals. Overall
confidence is high, due to the Heather Formation and Thief Zone ‘calibration points’.
The well encountered an oil-bearing Upper Hugin in heel and toe with good reservoir qualities similar to offset
wells. The total geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long. A total ~760 m MD in gross oil-
bearing productive Upper Hugin (zones 3.1-3.3) was encountered.
Non-productive Heather intervals were mainly due to predominantly sub-seismic faulting. Thief Zone intervals
were water-flooded as expected. A short upflank interval of water flooded Upper Hugin at 3528-3539 m MD (zone
3.2) was not repeated further down in the well.
Towards the toe, the well was dropped into water-flooded Thief Zone and Lower Hugin due to tool failure. TD was
set in the Sleipner Formation. An exit of the field through the main fault, as expected, was not observed.
With high oil saturation in both toe and heel, the well encountered upside volumes in the targeted Upper Hugin
zone. Pre-drill expectation was that the heel would be water filled as indicated by reservoir simulation and 4D
seismic, while mainly the downflank toe section would encounter undrained oil.
At ~ 4700 m MD / 3147 m MSL a marked drop in resistivity to <0.7 Ohm.m in the Lower Hugin may indicate that
the well penetrated the initial field OWC. Water-flooded zones just above show resistivities of approximately 1
Ohm.m, which are consistent with injected Utsira water (+/ produced water).

2.2 Seismic interpretation versus well results
Sonic logs (both compressional and shear) were acquired in the two pilot wells 15/9-F-11 T2 and 15/9-F-11 A.
Checkshots and VSP were acquired in 15/9-F-11 T2.
2.2.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect
Top and Base Hugin Formation depth prognosis in the North prospect were initially very uncertain. The seismic
reflectivity in this area is very poor in all datasets and together with the presence of an undrilled thick Upper
Jurassic package in this downfaulted segment the uncertainties on Top and Base Hugin Formation prognoses
were set to ±70 m TVD. Top Hugin Formation came in +29 m TVD deeper and Base Hugin Formation came in
+33 m TVD deeper than prognosis.
Checkshot pairs and VSP (normal incident and rig source) were acquired. The synthetic seismic from well logs
confirmed a poor seismic signal on Top Hugin Formation and a weak seismic signal on Base Hugin Formation
compared to Base Hugin Formation on the main field (Figure 2-5). Using checkshots as time depth curve a
deeper time interpretation of Top and Base Hugin Formation is initiated. This information has been implemented
in the updated maps. VSP image data is processed by Schlumberger and preliminary results indicate a more
northern placement of the large main bounding fault compared to initial seismic interpretation. These preliminary
observations have also been implemented in the updated maps. It was not observed any improvement of Top and
Base Hugin reflectors on the preliminary results. Final imaging results of VSP processing are estimated to be
delivered during November 2013.

2.2.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment
Seismic reflectivity is in general poor also in the Northwest segment. In some parts it is assumed to be a good
seismic signal on Base Hugin Formation, but it varies across the area, possibly due to faulting. Top Hugin
Formation interpretation is model driven due to Hugin Formation probably within tuning of BCU. In the initial
seismic interpretation both Top and Base Hugin Formation had uncertainties of -15/+55 m TVD.
Top Hugin came in +24 m TVD deeper and Base Hugin came in +48 m TVD deeper than prognosed. From
synthetic seismic it can be observed that Top Hugin Formation is within tuning of BCU (Top Draupne Formation)
and that Base Hugin Formation is a good seismic event and as expected from seismic modelling and
observations on the main field. The deeper observations of Top and Base Hugin Formation and the observation of
a thicker Hugin package lead to an update of Top and Base Hugin Formation maps in the Northwest segment.
Synthetic seismic and updated interpretations are shown in Figure 2-6.

2.2.3 15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field
In the F-11 B IOR producer Top Hugin Formation came in -7 m TVD, i.e shallower than prognosed depth
(uncertainty on depth estimate was ±20 m TVD). Sonic logging was neither planned nor performed in this high
deviated well. No major surprises were encountered during drilling of the reservoir section and the smaller faults
on the Volve main structure were targeted within the uncertainty span given upfront drilling. The well was planned
to drill through the main bounding fault to the East, but this fault was not reached on the prognosed location and
the TD of well were set in the Sleipner Formation. Based on this, the seismic interpretation was updated at the toe
of the well and the main bounding fault of the Volve Field is moved further East in the updated maps of Top and
Base Hugin Formation (Figure 2-7).

36” section
The inclination ranges in this section by 0.07°-0.10°, 0.35° (start-end, max).
26” section
When continue drilling from 224 m ROP was held back to max 10 m/h to keep vertical direction. At 273 m the
OnTrak inclination showed increasing inclination 0.71º. Gyro surveys on wireline approved that inclination.
Calculations in the Compass software show a technical collision when projecting to the bit (40 m). While drilling
ahead to 347 m extensive reaming took place to get back to vertical. It was not possible to turn the well around
into the opposite previous hole direction, instead it drifted closer to the two already drilled wells beneath a
separation factor of 1. The inclination ranges in this section by 0.2°-0.7°, 0.7° (start-end, max).
The section and plan views for this 347 m long wellbore are shown in Figure 2-9.

2.3.2 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect
26” section
The inclination ranges in this section by 0.0°-2.5°, 2.5° (start-end, max).
The Utsira Formation was entered at 887 m, where the inclination was build up to 24º. Rotating in order to
observe behaviour resulted in a 2º drop. The remaining of the Utsira sandstones was drilled in orienting mode in
an attempt to hold or build the inclination to 22º. Toolface control in Utsira Formation was ultimate difficult due to
intermittent hardness of the formation was encountered, resulting in an uncontrollable deviation. Entering the
Skade Formation the steering deteriorated even further. Very soft formations were encountered, making it
impossible to rotate through it (losing angle). Steering required a lot of effort and time.

17 ½” section
The inclination ranges in this section by 22.9°-36.3°, 36.3° (start-end, max).
The deviation was 22.9 º inclination and 278º azimuth at the start. The well trajectory was very far from the plan
due to having been unable in the previous 26'' section to build and turn in unconsolidated sandstones of the Utsira
and the Skade formations with a motor. To move progressively towards the original plan involved building
inclination from 24º-35.8° and turning left from 283º-253° azimuth by 1592 m at a rate of 2.5°/30 m. Then a
tangent section was drilled to 1699 m, followed by a drop and turn with 2.5°/30 m to 2128 m. Inclination was
dropped down to 4.6° and the well turned right to 339° azimuth. Finally, a continuous build and turn with 2.5°/30 m
was performed to TD at 2580 m where the inclination is 37° and the azimuth 69.6°. At TD the 17 1/2" section was
well placed with 1.36 m below and 0.46 m left of the planned well path.
8 ½” section
This almost 2000 m MD long section started with an inclination of 36.1º, built up to 82.0º over the first 450 m in the
Shetland Group, held tangent over 700 m and dropped back to 14.4º above reservoir. No turn was planned, thus
the well continued on the same 69.9º azimuth and 14.4º inclination for a further 200 m to TD. The initial build in
the Lista and Ty formations went without problems, the tool gave more than the theoretical calculated rate. This
changed dramatically in the Tor limestone, where it became extremely difficult to achieve the required 3.0º/30 m
build rate. With the AutoTrak tool in steer mode with 100% steer force, it was still necessary to cut back on the
flow rate and hold back ROP to 5 m/h to get it to build. Consequently, over approximately 160 m, the well path
dropped some 14 m below the line. This was recovered in the tangent section by increasing the sail angle from
80.2 to 82.0º. Stick/slip values increased in the Shetland Group. The drop from 80.0° to 14.0º inclination was
started from 3710 m. Initially the tool response was as expected, but in the lower Hod Formation and into the
Cromer Knoll Group the drop rate showed a large increase. Drilling continued to section TD without further
problems.
The section and plan views for the wellbore (from slot to TD at 4562 m MD) are shown in Figure 2-10.

2.3.3 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment
8 ½” section
Start inclination for the section was 37.6°, build to approximately 75° turning left and drop again to 39.9° at TD.
The sidetrack off the cement plug went without any issues. The planned wellpath could easily be followed after
that, with the AutoTrak capable of 7º/30 m. The drilling through the Tor Formation proved not to be that difficult
this time (compared with 15/9-F-11 T2). The tangent inclination was therefore reduced to 75º, to get closer to the
line again prior to start the drop interval at 3187 m. From around 3302 m (in the lower Hod Formation) onwards,
highly fluctuating near-bit inclination readings were seen. The drop rate became too high, and consequently the
drop force had to be reduced considerably to limit the drop in inclination. After passing this trouble zone
approximately 3400 m, drilling continued without any further directional problems. In the Tor and Hod limestones
the drilling parameters had to be varied regularly to limit the stick/slip levels.

8 ½” section (geosteering section)
Objectives
The IOR well 15/9-F-11 B was planned to be placed in the Upper Hugin zone to drain remaining reserves
between the production wells 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-14 and continuing downflank towards water injector 15/9-F-5.
The planned well path was already characterised by challenging steering, thus beside the main focus on
horizontal steering, also sufficient turn in azimuth to prolong the reservoir section as deep into the structure as
possible needed to be ensured.
Summary
Against interpretation from 4D seismic analysis and reservoir simulation the target zone within Upper Hugin
showed good oil saturation throughout the reservoir section. Estimates of true vertical thicknesses (TVT) indicate
thicker reservoir upflank than expected. TD was set at 4770 m MD, as the wellbore entered Sleipner Formation
below Hugin Formation, without penetrating the anticipated southwesterly field-bounding fault. The total
geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long. A total ~760 m MD in gross oil-bearing
productive Upper Hugin (zones 3.1-3.3) was encountered.
Geosteering
The pre-model from Baker Hughes (RNS) indicated higher contrast towards top reservoir, i.e. Heather Formation
and very low contrasts towards Thief Zone below. Additionally are several faults along the well path prognosed,
causing down-faulted reservoirs
For dip calculations the azimuthal GR (11 m behind bit) and azimuthal DEN (24 m behind bit) were used, also to
some extent distance to bed (D2B) calculations. Hence geosteering commands were affected by often significant
time delays. Several faults were crossed, causing 3 times an unexpected/non-pre-warned exit of top reservoir
(D2B ranged from 3,5-4,1 m TVD). Entering of the reservoir again was just estimated based on the geological
model and steering with maximum dogleg downwards. According to Baker Hughes’ RNS top reservoir cannot be
detected earlier than 1-2 m TVD from above. D2B calculations were not always applicable for bed boundary
detection, due to complex resistivity profile. Another 3 times the well continued from Upper Hugin into the
underlying Thief Zone (yellow circle). Here no distinct signal response was observed. Distances to bed were
roughly estimated to range from 1,5-2,0 m TVD.
Geosteering decisions were made by the Volve PETEK with support from GEOP and the Baker Hughes’ RNS
team. The RNS supervisor was present in the Statoil office in Bergen during office hours, while RNS support
outside office hours was covered by the Baker Hughes’ BEACON center in Stavanger.
Wellpath description
28 real-time geosteering decisions were performed. Any changes in trajectory was done to optimise wellbore
positioning in the reservoir and to avoid shaly and water-filled zones respectively.
- Started drilling reservoir section from 3198,0 m MD / 2783,8 m TVD. Increased inclination from 63° to 71°.
- Entered Heather Formation at 3351,6 m MD / 2846,6 m TVD. Possible fault with minor throw at 3414 m
MD, indicated by high angle dip. Possibly repetition of penetrated stratigraphy.

3.4 Electrical logging, VSP
Planning
In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 to Volve North prospect a vertical seismic profiling survey (VSP) was planned. Purpose
of the survey was:
- primary: more accurate time-depth relationship,
- secondary objective: improve imaging of reservoir and faults.
The feasibility study and pre-job modelling was performed by Thor Allan Garden (thgar@statoil.com, VSP group).
Based on previous results from 15/9-F-15 A and recent seismic data a walk-away survey with 2 sources was
recommended.
Schlumberger’s VSI-4 had been selected and after evaluation of operational risk, especially the highly deviated
well path (maximum 82° in open hole), finally a pump down VSP inside a simple BHA was selected.
Operations
A rig source VSP and a vertical incidence VSP (flip-flop configuration) were recorded in the 8 ½” open hole, the
14” and the 20” cased hole. The sources were 3x250 cu.in G-guns, and a Trisor OFS used for advanced gun
performance control.
Vertical incidence survey interval was recorded between 4530.0- 2228.8 m MD, the rig source survey 4530-582,9
m at 15 m intervals.
While RIH checkshots were taken at 1786,6 and 2518,4 m MD; a correlation pass was run from 4450 m up. While
POOH and measuring communication interference between rig and the vessel Dina Star were experienced at
2228,8 m. The survey was continued with the rig source only.
Results
Preliminary VSP imaging results indicated a more northern placement (~30-50 m) of the main bounding fault to
the north compared to the initial seismic interpretation. Final processing is pending.

3.5 Pressure points
3.5.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect
In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 at 6 depths pressures points in the water-filled Hugin reservoir were taken (Table 3-5).
This was performed while POOH after TD on May 04th and 5th 2013. At this time both water injectors (15/9-F-4
and 15/9-F-5) were in operation. 6 of 6 tests were successful and of good technical quality (all reached a stable
pressure).
Pressure points were showing a reservoir pressure 13-14 bar over initial, relative to 15/9-19 BT2 and 15/9-F-15 A
water gradient, but in accordance with the main field pressure, which was ~12 bar over initial at the time (Figure
3-1).

3.5.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment
In pilot well 15/9-F-11 A all in all 24 pressure stations in the oil-bearing Hugin Formation were taken. 17 (+2
repeated) were performed while POOH after TD on May 21st 2013 (Table 3-6). At this time both water injectors
(15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5) were in operation. On May 23th 2013 additional 5 pressure measurements were repeated.
This time after both water injectors were shutdown over a period of almost 48 hours. 18 of 24 tests (2 sets of data
with time delay) were successful and of good technical quality (one might be supercharged). 6 tests were tight.
Formation pressure points in the Hugin Formation were 12 bar above initial formation pressure in oil zone
(measured in 15/9-F-12, February 2008) (Figure 3-1). This indicates communication with the Hugin Formation in
the Volve main field, since reservoir pressure has been increased ~12 bar above initial pressure by injection at
the time.
Time lapse repeated pressure points (Figure 3-1) after closing in main field injections were 0.2-0.9 bar lower than
48hrs earlier. At the same time a pressure drop of 6 bar was recorded in the main field pressure gauge in well
15/9-F-15 C. The small pressure drop was consistent with prognosis from the simulation model.
