Objectives
In both pilot wells optional fluid sampling was planned.
- 15/9-F-11 (T2): 1-2 oil samples for compositional analysis, density and viscosity & 1 water sample for
resistivity measurements as water saturation reference for the petrophysical model as well compositional
analysis, density, viscosity were planned to take, if the Hugin Formation in North prospect is oil filled or an
oil-water contact is been encountered.
- 15/9-F-11 A: 1 water sample for compositional analysis, density and viscosity to define water type (initial
formation water or perched water), if the Hugin Formation in Northwest segment is partly oil-bearing.
Since the open hole wellpaths of both 8 ½” pilot sections were planned with inclinations of 82° in 15/9-F-11 (T2)
and 75° in 15/9-F-11 A respectively a feasibility study by Schlumberger recommended pipe conveyed wireline
logging for running MDT.
Summary
In none of the pilot wells the criteria were achieved. Well 15/9-F-11 T2 found water-filled the Hugin Formation in
the North prospect/segment, whereas well 15/9-F-11 A proved the entire Hugin reservoir oil-bearing in the
Northwest segment. Therefore all MDT runs were cancelled.

5 Pore pressures and hole stability
5.1 Formation pressure above the reservoir
The prediction of the pore pressure gradient (PP) above the reservoir is based on the Eaton Sonic method.
- A hydrostatic pore pressure gradient is expected down to top Ty Formation. The pore pressure within
permeable Ty sandstones was measured in wells 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-15 S as 0.87 sg. This indicates
some 40 bar pressure depletion caused by production from the Sleipner Øst Field in the South. Recent
pressure data from Sleipner well 15/9-A-24, the last gas condensate producer, show stable pressure since
2005, in line with the stop of production in the northern Sleipner Øst area. The pore pressure in the Ty
Formation within the Volve area is expected to be 0.87 sg, as measured in 2007.
- In the underlying upper part of the Shetland Group the pore pressure is assumed to increase gently up to
1.05 sg within the Hod Formation. For the rest of the Shetland and the Cromer Knoll Group a rapid increase
in the pore pressure to 1.26 sg is prognosed.
- The maximum pore pressure within the Viking Group is expected to be 1.29 sg in the organic rich Draupne
shales at the top. The pore pressure within the underlying Heather Formation is expected to be lower.
5.2 Pore pressure in the reservoir
The reservoir pressure in the Hugin Formation main field is currently maintained at approximately 10-12 bar over
initial pressure by injecting water downflank in wells 15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5. Throughout the field an oil gradient of
0,0704 bar/m and a water gradient of 0.103 bar/m is assumed. Pressure measurements were undertaken in both
pilot wells (Chapter 3.5).
- In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 pressure range was measured to be 13-14 bar over initial in the water zone.
- Pilot well 15/9-F-11 A proved a pressure of 12 bar above initial formation pressure in oil zone.
- For IOR well 15/9-F-11 B the pressure in the Hugin Formation has been estimated to be 12 bar over initial
(June 2013), referring to time equivalent pressure measurements at downhole pressure gauges in Volve
main field.
At the base of the Heather Formation likely bad to moderate sandstones in the North prospect and Northwest
segment are expected to be present. Even there is not often interpreted flow potential at all; this zone has been
anticipated to correspond to reservoir pressure.

A minimum horizontal strength gradient has been calculated from a Breckels and van Eekelen relation based
on a method from Per Horsrud (2011). Initial reservoir pressure is used.
- Formation strength in the Balder Formation is reduced due to the presence of tuff layers. Nevertheless
XLOT data from well 15/9-F-14 showed a minimum horizontal stress gradient (ShG) of 1.68 sg in the upper
part of the Balder Formation. In the lower 32 m of the Balder Formation the formation strength is reduced to
1.55 sg to account for local experiences.
- Within the Ty reservoir the formation strength is assumed to be reduced due to depletion, but should not
cause any hole stability problems. It is well above the anticipated pore pressure in the overburden section.
- The Minifrac data from the Hugin Formation in well 15/9-19 A was revaluated. The result is a 19 bar
reduction in the minimum horizontal stress gradient for the Hugin reservoir and the formations below.
The fracture gradient for the Volve area is set as 5% above the minimum horizontal strength, according to the
model.
- In the upper part of the Balder Formation an adjustment to only 1.73 sg was done to account for reduced
formation strength due to the presence of tuff layers.
A collapse gradient has been generated based on the method of Stassi d’Alia. Included in this method are the
initial pore pressure, the minimum horizontal stress, considerations of the UCS (unconfined compressive
strength), and the well path.
- The collapse curve in the Nordland Group and upper Hordaland Group shows a general increase towards
the hydrostatic pore pressure and surpasses it in the Skade Formation. The 36” & 26” sections were drilled
with 1.03 sg seawater/spud mud successfully.
- The Hordaland shales are normally associated with high smectite content and under-compaction, causing
instability and possibly higher pore pressure in the lower Hordaland Group. However, in the Volve area the
sandstones of the Grid Formation are generally present, acting as a pressure vent and thereby preventing
abnormal pore pressure. Nevertheless, shales within the Hordaland Group have not been observed as
unstable, when drilled at high inclinations and particularly when exposed to water based drilling fluid.
The UCS has been updated/increased by a factor of 1.2 while planning the 2nd drilling campaign
o
and re-evaluating earlier results. Positive experiences from well 15/9-F-5, where the lower
Hordaland and the Rogaland Group have been drilled with 1.35 sg OBM at inclinations of 27°-31°
without any incidents and on the other hand tight hole conditions in well 15/9-F-12 with an
inclination <10° also with 1.35 sg OBM were taken into account.
A maximum collapse gradient of 1.34 sg is estimated (maximum 23° inclination). No incidents have been
observed when drilling with 1.40 sg standard OBM.
- A peak in the collapse gradient is calculated at the base of the Lista Formation. There is no definite
evidence for significant unstable conditions in this interval, but washouts were observed on MWD logs and
sticky problems occurred when running casing on some previous wells. However post-drilling the UCS was
adjusted/increased by a factor 1.15 to account for no incidents when drilling this lower part with 1.28 sg
standard OBM, and several kick-off from the here placed casing shoe.
- No incidents have been observed in the Cromer Knoll Group.

6 Petrophysical evaluation
6.1 Summary
6.1.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect
The Hugin Formation was water-filled, except of two pockets of residual oil that seem to be structurally trapped.
(Lowest Sw achieved in these is ~ 0.4) One residual oil pocket is situated below the Heather Formation and the
other below the “offshore shale” which separates Upper Hugin from the “Thief Zone” (Hugin zone 2).
These 2 residual oil pockets are observed in the following intervals:
4393.8-4399 m MD / 3182.3-3187.3 m MSL,
4415.8-4419.7 m MD / 3203.6-3207.4 m MSL.
An oil show is described on cuttings @ 4411 m MD.
The rest of the Hugin Formation, which was water-filled, showed very low resistivities < 0.05 Ohm.m in the best
sands, is reflecting the very saline formation water on Volve (~ 130000 ppm). This might indicate that the Hugin
Formation never has been oil-filled, and that the two observed oil pockets are results of hydrocarbon migration.
The Hugin Formation properties seem to be poorer than encountered in previous Volve wells, with an average
porosity of 0.17. This is 4 p.u. less than the porosity in the Hugin Formation in the exploration well 15/9-19 BT2,
which is the closest well with respect to TVD depth. Net/Gross is however high as expected (Hugin > 0.8); in this
well 0.81.
Porosity from MagTrak is a lithology independent porosity, and MagTrak porosity in 15/9-F-11 T2 confirms in
general the porosity from the density log.
Formation pressure points in the Hugin Formation were 13.7 bar above initial formation pressure measured in
15/9-19 BT2 (January 1998). This indicates communication with the Hugin Formation in the Volve main field,
since reservoir pressure has been increased ~12 bar above initial pressure by injection, prior to this drilling
campaign.
LWD log data is of good quality.
No oil and water samples were taken since no commercial find.

6.1.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment
The Hugin Formation was oil-filled, with porosity being slightly lower and water saturation being higher than
previous Hugin wells. An average porosity of 0.21 in 15/9-F-11 A is 2 p.u. less than the porosity in the Hugin

Net/Gross is however as expected > 0.8, in this well 0.86.
An oil-down to situation (ODT) ~ 3702 m MD / 3025.5 m MSL was proven, which depth corresponds to Top
Sleipner Formation.
Formation pressure points in the Hugin Formation were 12 bar above initial formation pressure measured in 15/9-
F-12 (February 2008). This indicates communication with the Hugin Formation in the Volve main field, since
reservoir pressure has been increased ~12 bar above initial pressure by injection, prior to this drilling campaign.
To get a picture of the time lapse effect, injection was closed, and a second set of formation pressure (only 5
depth stations were repeated) was logged 48 hours after the first formation pressure data set. This showed only
minor decrease in formation pressure, in the range: 0.2-0.9 bar.
LWD log data is of good quality.
No water sample was taken since water was not encountered below the oil column.
6.1.3 15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field
The Hugin Formation was oil-filled in large parts of the well, and encountered also oil in the first part (shallowest)
of the Hugin Formation, which was not expected from 4D and simulation model. Averages for the Hugin
Formation are given in Chapter 6.6.1 to 6.6.3.
Flooded intervals are given in Table 6-5. A flag curve showing flooded intervals is named FLOODED_FLAG (in
flooded intervals is FLOODED_FLAG = 1), and plotted as turquoise intervals in track no. 8 in the CPI plot.
Below ~ 4701 m MD / 3148 m MSL (a coal layer) the water seems to be initial, due to the low resistivity. This is
plotted as dark blue interval in track no. 8 in the CPI plot. Hence 15/9-F-11 B might indicate a WUT = 3148 m
MSL, which is not in conflict with the observed ODT (3145 m MSL) in the main field.
Since there is a huge contrast between the very saline Hugin Formation water and the fresh, low saline Utsira
Formation (injection) water, 3 cases with different formation water salinity are presented in the 15/9-F-11 B CPI in
order to illustrated the impact on water saturation. The details of these cases are given in the header of the CPI.
LWD log data is of good quality.

Note in general that Baker considers the GRAFM curve as the best GR reading for large hole sizes > 17 ½” hole.
The reason is as follows: GRCFM is corrected for sensor type, collar size, carbonate content in mud, mud weight
and hole size. GRAFM is only corrected for sensor type, collar size and carbonate content in mud. The hole size

6.2.2 Depth shifts, splicing
No depth shifting has been applied, except in limited overlap intervals around kick off depths and hole sections in
Overburden. These depth shifts and the splicing of the individual runs are reported in Logtek’s info file for each
well; WLC_COMPOSITE_1_INF_1.PDF, which is stored in Petrobank. All depths are referenced to driller’s depth.
Logtek’s general “philosophy” with respect to depth shifting in the transition zone between a mother well and a
kick off well is as follows: aim to keep the stop-coupled depths as shallow as possible, preferably at KOP. But
sometimes it has to be set deeper (a few meters deeper, preferably in a shale if possible) due to a) if overlapping
curves are not on depth at KOP, or b) if the depth shifts have been so large, that the logs else will be too
compressed. This “philosophy” also applies of course for depth shifting in overlap between hole sections.

6.3 Petrophysical evaluation method and input parameters
6.3.1 General
Petrophysical evaluation is performed according to the Volve petrophysical field model, described in report:
“Sleipner Øst and Volve Model 2006, Hugin and Skagerrak Formation, Petrophysical Evaluation”. November
2006. Author: Elin Solfjell, Karl Audun Lehne.
The petrophysical evaluation software used is Geolog, and the Geolog project is SLEIPNER_OST (at Stavanger
server: FROST_SVG).
6.3.2 Porosity
φ
Total porosity, PHIF ( F), is derived from the density log, which is calibrated to overburden corrected core

VSH is used quantitatively in deriving permeability, and quantitatively indirectly in Netsand cutoff.
In order to pick GR and GR in a more objective way, and to avoid over-estimating of VSH, GR is
min max

reads higher porosity than the density, is this believed to be due to the hydrogen content of present clay minerals.

6.3.5 Permeability
The horizontal log permeability, KLOGH, is derived from the following equation based on multivariable regression
analysis between log porosity and shale volume (“normalized” against VSHDN) against overburden corrected
core permeability:
Hugin Formation: KLOGH =10(2+8×PHIF−9×VSH))
Sleipner Formation: KLOGH =10(−3+32×PHIF−2×VSH))
Skagerrak Formation: KLOGH =10(−1.85+17.4×PHIF−3×VSH))

6.4 Flag curves
Netsand flag
Cut-offs on PHIF and VSH are used to determine Netsand; SAND_FLAG = 1.
Hugin Formation: SAND_FLAG = 1 when PHIF > 0.10 and VSH < 0.50.
These cut offs correspond to a overburden corrected core permeability of 0.5 mD, and are also applied for the
other formations. In addition some manual editing might have been performed on the SAND_FLAG curve.
Carbonate flag
A carbonate flag curve, CARB_FLAG, has been determined manually by visual inspection of the logs. Whenever
CARB_FLAG = 1 is SAND_FLAG = 0.
Coal flag
15/9-F-11 T2: 2 coal layers were observed in the Sleipner Formation.
15/9-F-11 A: No coal layers were observed.
15/9-F-11 B: 2 coal layers were observed in the Hugin Formation.
The coal flag curve, COAL_FLAG, has been determined by a manually decided cut off on the density log. In

6.5 Nuclear magnetic resonance logging (LWD)
In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 Baker’s LWD NMR tool, MagTrak, was run in the 8 ½” section. The tool was ran in silent
mode, hence data only in memory. MagTrak sensor offset to bit was 50.45 m.
6.5.1 Logging mode and parameters
The tool was logged in “Dual Wait Time” (DTW) mode, also called “PoroPermMT+Light HC” mode, in order to
process a salinity independent water saturation.
An average ROP of 15 m/h was held in the reservoir interval to assure a sufficient vertical resolution of the
MagTrak data, and also to keep the signal/noise ratio high. Vertical resolution with DTW mode and ROP = 20 m/h
was calculated to be 1.7 m. Vertical resolution with DTW mode and ROP = 30 m/h was calculated to be 2.6 m.
The MagTrak data was logged and final post processed with the following parameters:
• running average (data stacking) of 16: RA = 16
• T2 cutoffs: CBW = 3.3 ms, BVI = 33 ms (standard sandstone T2 cut-offs)
• number of T2 bins: 27 over the range of 0.5-4096 ms.

6.5.3 MagTrak purpose and achievements
Purpose with running MagTrak was to achieve:
• lithology independent porosity,
• independent (semi-quantitative) permeability indicator, possibility of detecting thief zone,
• salinity independent water/oil saturation,
• discriminate between movable and bound fluid,
• irreducible water saturation,
• independent estimation of saturation exponent, n,
• pore size distribution.
Since the reservoir turned out to be more or less water filled, some of the purposes were not any longer
applicable.
Achievements
• MagTrak provides both a total and an effective porosity which is lithology independent. Total porosity from
MagTrak, MPHS, is plotted (magenta curve) in same track as total porosity, PHIF, from conventional logs
in the F-11 T2 CPI. The overall trend in the Hugin Formation is that the MPHS match well with PHIF,
which should not be a surprise in a water-filled reservoir. MPHS has (and shows) however a poorer
vertical resolution than PHIF.
• In the Heather Formation PHIF is lower than MPHS, indicating perhaps a too low matrix density applied
for this formation.
• MagTrak provides an independent semi-quantitative permeability indicator, MPERM. This curve is plotted
(magenta curve) in same track as the permeability from conventional logs and field model, KLOGH, in the
15/9-F-11 T2 CPI. The overall trend is that MPERM > KLOGH with the applied standard sandstone T2
cut-offs.
• The thief zone is not detected by MPERM. From the T2 distribution (Figure 6-2) it is however seen that
the sand in interval 4449-453 m MD (lower Thief Zone / Hugin 2.1 (7)) has the complete T2 signal coming
from the oil-based mud filtrate, hence indicating qualitatively the most permeable zone of this Hugin
Formation. Invasion will however of course be dependent of different factors, such as mud properties and
ROP.
• SCAL data from 15/9-19 A was investigated to check if the default coefficients for the Coates-Timur
permeability (MPERM) equation were appropriate (Chapter 6.5.4). This resulted in a permeability curve
called MPERM_ADJUSTED which is plotted (light green curve) in the permeability track (track no. 9) in
the 15/9-F-11 T2 CPI.
• Since the reservoir was more or less water filled, it was no point to order a processing from Baker Hughes
to get independent oil saturation.
• MagTrak provides discrimination between movable and bound fluid. This is illustrated in track no. 8 in the
15/9-F-11 T2 CPI. Brown shading = clay bound fraction, turquoise shading = capillary bound fraction and
yellow shading = free or movable fluid fraction.

6.5.4 Coates-Timur permeability
A verification/check of the coefficients in the Coates-Timur permeability equation has been done.
Due to lack of cores in well 15/9-F-11 T2, the SCAL data in well 15/9-19 A was reviewed. An overview over SCAL
permeability versus irreducible water saturation from different methods is given in Figure 6-3.
Figure 6-3 SCAL data from well 15/9-19 A
All permeability data versus irreducible water saturation data derived from different methods listed to the right and
separated by different colours. The data from mercury injection should be disregarded.
Data from the CCAL (Conventional Core AnaLysis) plugs are shown as purple squares in Figure 6-3. The water
saturation is measured by Dean and Stark method (OBM) and is measured together with porosity at vertical
plugs. Permeability is however not measured on same vertical plugs, but on horizontal plugs. Due to this,
permeability has been picked from adjacent plugs with approximately same porosity.

Remarks regarding well 15/9-F-11 B CPI
Note that for well 15/9-F-11 B is the CPI separated in 3 parts for better visualisation.
Note also that the 15/9-F-11 B CPIs include 3 different formation water salinity cases:
• First case is Hugin Formation water salinity, second case is a mixture of Hugin and Utsira Formation
water salinity, third case is pure Utsira Formation water salinity.
The details in difference and colour coding are explained in the header of the CPI’s. There is a huge contrast
between the very saline Hugin Formation water and the fresh, low saline Utsira Formation water (injection water),
and the different cases illustrate the impact on the water saturation.
Note also that the salinity is changed in the whole evaluated intervals, and that all other parameters are kept
unchanged.

of F-11 well slot with top of fish approximately flush with top of F-11 guide funnel (Top of Fish
(TOF) at ca. 140,20 mRKB).

Synergi 1351767 (Closed) 16.03.2013 – non-conformities – Equipment failure Baker MWD
At approximately 17:30 the failed Ontrack tool was at surface and broking out. At this point seal
on OnTrack was seen to be out between modular stabulizer and OnTrack. Stopped the job.
DD advised that this could be caused by a leaking/exploded lithium batteri.
Barriered off rig floor. Evaluated situation and discussed situation w/ Baker onshore organisation.
Evaluated situation and discoused same with onshore organisation.
1351767 4 Held SJA meeting with offshore management team and Baker onshore HSE dep.
Formulated plan:
1. Check tool with temp. and gas measurements, wearing full PPE and BA set
2. Install lifting sub on top and B/O tool below leaving sub below attached, effectively sealing the
tool from both sides
3. L/O the tool and take temp. and gas readings again
4. Tool to be stored in basket on deck and check measured every 2nd hour for temp. and gas for
12 hours.

Synergi 1353976 (Closed) 17.03.2013 – non-conformities – Increasing inclination trend while
drilling 26" section discovered to late
Drilling of the 26" interval on the 15/9-F-11 well commenced on the 15.03.2013, 18:00 with
drilling the 30" shoe track to 202 m and cleaning the rathole down to 208 m.
Two gyro wireline single shot surveys were taken inside the conductor at 147,7 (0,21deg) and
167,7 (0,29deg) as the36" section had been drilled on inclination only.
Drilling continued after midnigth on the 16.03. from 208 m to 210 m when communication to the
MWD (OnTrak) was lost. GyroTrak surveys could not be obtained yet due to string movement in
open sea.
Drilled ahead to 215 m when operations had to be interrupted due to a dropped object from the
topdrive. After a PDO check drilling continued to 224 m while troubleshooting on the MWD tool
was performed unsuccessful. Decision was made to POOH.
GyroTrak surveys failed the QA/QC criteria due to steady string movement induced by rough
sea. No wireline gyro was run due to the extended red zone after dropped object and heavy
1353976 3
vibrations from the drill string.
On the 17.03. 13:00 the BHA was back on bottom and drilling continued. The directional driller
used the OnTrak continuous inclination measurement as GyroTrak surveys were still not passing
the QA/QC criteria. The continuous Inc. showed a dropping trend according to the DD.
At around 21:00 wireline was rigged up and a gyro run with the following stations was
performed:
Survey depth: 224,6 m 0,50 deg Inc.
224,6 m 0,51 deg Inc.
212,0 m 0,44 deg Inc.
202,0 m 0,27 deg Inc.
193,0 m 0,34 deg Inc.
180,0 m 0,20 deg Inc.
Despite the increasing trend in inclination read from the gyro surveys, drilling continued.
At 23:00 another gyro wireline run was performed, bit depth was 282 m.
Survey depth: 238,7 m 0,59 deg Inc.
Synergi 1351926 (Closed) 18.03.2013 – non-conformities – Leak on top drive IBOP
1351926 4 Observed leak on top drive IBOP. Pulled back into 30" conductor shoe.
Lowered top drive to rig floor
Synergi 1352079 (Closed) 22.03.2013 – non-conformities – Alignment through top drive to DP
was not straight
1352079 4 When inspected the top drive visually, it was detected that the alignment through the top drive
and down to the drill pipe when screwed in was not straight. With a closer investigation, it was
discovered that the pipe guide hanging underneath the top drive was tilted forward in an angle.
As we were approaching a long 26” section we could not start the drilling as it was.

Synergi 1357394 (Closed) - 24.04.2013 - non-conformities - Draw works brake encoder load
transfer torque failure
0600 hrs the ET was called to the floor because the driller faced problems with draw work.
Investigation showed that the draw work motors torque up attempting to take the load from the
draw work brake, but when the break released the casing lowered 2-3 inches and the brake
hooked in again. Alarm : ¿LOAD transfer torque failure¿ occurred
At the moment we only have 3 draw work motors available due to a VFD fault on motor A. The
remaining 3 motors only torque up to 46 % before the brake was released and then the casing
lowered as described.
The driller lowered the casing step by step (hard trip) until they were in correct position to nipple
1357394 3
up for mud circulation and be able to connect a new stand.
Similar problem in 2005. We found the handover from 2005 describing the problem and also with
a attached recommendation from Varco.
All drilling activity was stopped and we shut down all DWS servers for trying to restart the
system. The restart did not solve the problem.
The parameter described in 2005 by Varco (Torque correction factor) was checked and we tried
to increase the value as recommended up 1,5 (original setting was 1,2). This did not solve the
problem either.
It was decided to continue the casing job again 1230 hrs without using the draw work brake. We
could make the torque transfer when the hook load was below 250 T

Synergi 1351677 (Closed) - 09.Mar.2013 - Non-conformities - String broken in 36" section
ROV moved towards F-11 well centre to observe POOH and observe 36" hole opener assy had detached from string.
ROV checked template and observed 36" hole opener fish sticking up out of F-11 well slot with top of fish
approximately flush with top of F-11 guide funnel (Top of Fish (TOF) at ca. 140,20 mRKB).
Description:
After waiting on weather for many hours it was decided to RIH for a wiper trip. There was a drop in hookload as the
string got stuck and when continued RIH the string started to bend. ROV moved towards F-11 well centre to observe
POOH and observe 36" hole opener assy had detached from string. ROV checked template and observed 36" hole
opener fish sticking up out of F-11 well slot with top of fish approximately flush with top of F-11 guide funnel (Top of
Fish (TOF) at ca. 140,20 mRKB).
Immediate solution: Mobilized fishing equipment and prepared for fishing operation
Future recommended solution: When drilling top hole (without riser) always keep neutral point below seabed. Have
available fishing equipment, both for service companies and Mærsk equipment.

Synergi 1351759 (Closed) - 24.Apr.2013 - Non-conformities - Dropped object.
Description:
Drilled 30" conductor shoe and cleaned 17"X26"x36" rat hole below conductor with 26" motor BHA.
Continued drilling from bottom of rat hole at 208 m to 215 m. Heavy vibrations in string transfered to the top drive
during drilling and reaming.
It should also be noted that rough sea created lateral movement of the string while drilling.
BHA in use was drilling run #2
Parameters:
4500 l/min, 60 - 60 rpm string rotation,130 - 135 bar, 2-9 KNm
The following incidents could be caused by the heavy vibrations:

Immediate solution: Reinstalled the guide and bolts torqued as per bolt satisfaction.
Future recommended solution: Due to technical sidetrack the section will be kicked off again below the 30"
conductor. As a mitigation measure, the BHA is planned to include 9 1/2" DC as replacement for 8 1/4" DC.

Subject: Running 14" casing - several improvements identified on AAR
Section: NO 15/9-F-11 T2, 17 1/2"
Rep date: 24.Apr.2013
Keywords: CASING/LINER
Downtime: Pot time improvements:
Comp inv: Weatherford Norge AS
References:
Synergi no: Cost:
Synergi desc:
Description:
After running of 14" casing on the F-11 T2 well Maersk Drilling performed an After Action Review.
The following improvements were identified:
- During the first phase of running casing, the driller used the "drill" function on the link tilt to clear the BX elevator
from the casing. Then the bails were tilted out to pick up the next joint.
When using the "drill" function the tilt arms are pushed against the bails (compared to "tilt" which pulls on the bails).
This stress resulted in nuts from u-clamps coming loose and dropping to the floor inside the red zone (impact 5 joule).
The running sequence was revised and the dolly retract was used to clear the top drive/elevators from the casing string.
- Weatherford BX elevator need to have correct rotator, it should be supplied with the casing running equipment
package
- Ensure stabbing guides and telescopic arms are adjusted correctly on Weatherford casing tong frame. Adjustments
should be done offline prior to R/U
- Remove one FMS topguide while running shoetrack to allow centralizers to pass through
- Orientate eye bolts on BX inserts so that they run parallel with the bails and don't interfere with the casing couplings
- The threads are prepared for Baker lock on the pipe deck, when lifting them up use a celan protector cap instead of a
rag to protect the threads.
Immediate solution: Follow up with Weatherford
Future recommended solution: Recommended to get the equipment list sent offshore prior to sending equipment
offshore. If this is done before the operation takes place, we can check what equipment is compatible with the rig
equipment.
Classification: Internal Status: Final Expiry date: Page 16 of 168

Follow up with Weatherford Future recommended solution: Recommended to get the equipment list sent offshore prior to sending equipment offshore. If this is done before the operation takes place, we can check what equipment is compatible with the rig equipment. Classification: Internal Status: Final Expiry date: Page 16 of 168

4 Activity highlights
4.1 NO 15/9-F-11, 36" (07.Mar.2013 00:00)
START: 07.Mar.2013 00:00 146 m MD
END : 14.Mar.2013 20:00 208 m MD
OBJECTIVE
Spud and drill 36” section to 208 m MD/208 m TVD.
Run and cement 30” conductor to seabed.
SUMMARY
The well was spudded, and a 36” hole was drilled from 146 m MD to section TD 208 m MD in one run with an
average rate of penetration (ROP) of 5.7 m/hr. The hole was drilled with a 36” x 26” hole opener above a 17 ½” bit. At
TD 25 m3 sweep was pumped and a wiper trip was performed and the well displaced to 1.40 sg spud mud.
After several hours of waiting on weather (WOW) the bit was run back to bottom to check for fill and displaced again
with 1.40 sg mud. When on surface found that BHA had parted with top of fish at F-11 template funnel. Recovered
115,91 m of BHA to surface. ROV performed template survey with poor visibility and found a section of BHA laying
on sea bed next to template.
Evaluated situation and investigated further to determine details of fish in hole and laying on sea bed. Using a serial
no. observed on top of fish at template funnel identified that the upper most part of the fish was a 9 11/16" OD wear
sleeve which forms the upper part of 11 3/4" stabilizer on the OnTrack tool.
Length of fish in hole re-calculated to be 11,90 m, approximately 4-5 ton (in air).
Length of BHA on seabed estimated to be 46.62 m. approximately 7 ton (in air).
M/U fishing BHA spear assembly to attempt to engage 3" ID opening at top of fish. POOH and M/U 2 1/2" - 3 5/8"
Taper Tap BHA. The fish was successfully engaged and fish was layed out on seabed.
A new 36” BHA was M/U and RIH. The hole was circulated clean and displaced to 1.40 sg mud, before the BHA was
pulled to surface and laid down.
The conductor was then run to 202 m MD and cemented to surface without incidents. The conductor was held in place
while the cement set up.
BIT RUNS
RUN # 1
A 17 1/2” Hughes Christensen VM-T303DX0 type bit on an Odfjell 26” x 36”, two stage hole opener (HO) and a
Baker Hughes MWD string was run in hole on 5 ½” HWDP and 5 ½” drill pipe (DP) and drilled to TD in one run.
Drilling Parameters: 3400 lpm, 77 bar, 62m, 10.8 drill hrs, 14.8 circ hrs, 5.7 m/hr, 2 – 3 tons, 60 – 70 rpm, 4 - 8 kNm
Bit grading: N/A (on seabed)
Classification: Internal Status: Final Expiry date: Page 28 of 168

A 26” Hughes Christensen VG-03M type bit together with a 36” hole opener and a Baker MWD string was run in hole
on 5 ½” drill pipe (DP) and cleaned hole.
Parameters: 1000lpm, 4 bar, 16m, 1,2 drill hrs, 9 circ hrs, 10.8 m/hr, 1 – 2 tons, 10 rpm, 1 - 2 kNm
Bit grading: 1 – 1 – WT – A – E – I – NO – DTF
Drilled cement 12m for 1:42hrs.
DRILLING FLUIDS
The section was drilled with 1.03 sg Seawater / Polymer spudmud and hivis sweeps. Two 10 m³ pills were generally
pumped per stand. At TD the hole was cleaned with a 25 m³ hivis pill. A wipertrip was performed and the well was
displaced to 1.40 sg seawater/ bentonite mud.
MWD/BHA
A Baker MWD BHA was included for directional control.
CASING
The 2 upper joints, 30”, X-65, 456.6lbs RL-4 HC and the 3 lower joints, X-52, 309.7lbs, ST-2 FB conductor was run
to casing depth, at 202 m MD on 5 ½” HWDP, without incidents.
CEMENTING
The 30” conductor was cemented with 1.55 sg lead Tuned Light XL blend through the 5 ½” HWDP landing string.
The cement was planned to seabed.

4.2 NO 15/9-F-11, 26" (14.Mar.2013 20:00)
START: 14.Mar.2013 20:00 208 m MD
END : 20.Mar.2013 09:30 347 m MD
OBJECTIVE
Drill and orient 26” hole to section TD at The 20” casing is set below Skade Fm to seal off Utsira Fm and Skade Fm.
SUMMARY
Ran in hole and drilled out the shoetrack from 197 to 202 m MD. The rat hole was cleaned out and reamed in stages to
208m MD. No restrictions were verified by pulling through the rat hole and the shoe, before commenced drilling.
Drilled 26” hole to 281 m MD, evaluated survey data and wellpath. Reamed hole from 212 to 281m MD and evaluated
new survey data. Recalculated projected wellpath and drilled from 281 m to 347 m MD. Decided to plug back 26”
open hole from 347 m to 202 m MD due to close proximit and pulled out of hole (POOH). There was also a MWD
tool failure on this run that made it necessary to change out most of the MWD part of the BHA.
BIT RUNS

OBJECTIVE
Prove oil in North prospect and define the field OWC
SUMMARY
The section started with a 12 ¼” cleanout assembly for drilling out the 14” shoetrack. The cement was tagged at 2541
m MD and whilst drilling the shoetrack and 3 m of new formation the mud weight was cut from 1.42 sg to 1.28 sg.
Performed the FIT to 1.65 sg EMW.
P/U the 8 ½” BHA and drilled to 2905 m MD before the ROP was reduced due to problems with building angle.
Drilled to 3278 m MD with reduced ROP.
Drilling continued to 3800 m MD, and background LCM concentration were increased when minor losses were
observed. Started to increase MW to 1.32 sg during two circulations. Reached TD at 4562 m MD without any
problems. Circulated hole clean with 3 x B/U while reciprocating the string.
Performed relogging and took pressure points with TesTrak on different depths while POOH. L/D the BHA and
picked up new 8 ½” BHA for VSP on wireline inside drillpipe. RIH and tagged TD at 4562 m MD and circulated B/U.
POOH with VSP BHA and rigged up for Gyro wireline and RIH to TD, Performed gyro wireline from 145m MD to
4562 m MD. Broke out and laid down gyro wireline toolstring. Started to POOH with 5 ½” drillpipe, but observed
restrictions on several depths. Attemped to pass restriction with 30MT – no go. Established circulation, got pass
restriction and pulled the string to surface.
Rigged up and RIH with cement stinger to 4160 m MD, broke circulation and washed down to TD. Cement plug # 1
was set from 4562-4160 m MD in two stages. The cement stinger was pulled out to 2571 m MD to wait for cement to
set. Meanwhile the BOP was tested and MW reduced from 1.32 sg to 1.28 sg. Top of plug # 1 was tagged with 10 MT
at 4104 m MD and a HiVis pill pumped prior to setting the KO plug.
Pulled out of hole and prepared to sidetrack 8 ½” pilot to North West.

OBJECTIVE
Sidetrack and drill 12 ¼” hole from 14” casing shoe and perform a gyro verification run when POOH to 14” casing
shoe.
SUMMARY
This section was drilled in two runs. A 12 ¼” rotary steerable drilling BHA was picked up and RIH in cased hole.
Tagged top of the second cement plug at 2531m MD. The cement inside the casing was drilled while treating the mud.
Prior to entering the Ty Fm. A 4m3/stand 1.28 sg LCM pill was bled into the system to prevent any losses. When
entering the Shetland chalk, the ROP reduced from 19 m/hr to 11 m/hr and then finally to 5 m/hr. After slow drilling
(13 m for 25 hrs) it was decided to POOH for a bit change.
The mud motor, CoPilot, AutoTrak and the bit were layed out. These components were replaced prior to RIH. TD was
reached at 3197 m MD without any problems, hole circulated clean with 3.5 x B/U while reciprocating the string.
A gyro was dropped before pulling the BHA to surface.
A 9 5/8” x 10 ¾” casing was run and cemented 400m above casing shoe. Displaced the cement with calculated
displacement volume plus 1/2 shoetrack volume. Total displacement volume with rig pumps 116 m3 (5227 strokes).
No indication of top plug bumped. Total losses while displacement 7-9 m3. Bleed off pressure and checked for back
flow. Monitored for back flow on trip tank. Recorded 1.2 m3 back flow over a periode of 1 hr. Closed full open safety
(FOSV) valve and monitored annulus on trip tank. L/D cement head and checked for back flow. Observed flow from
string.
Closed FOSV and installed circulation swedge. Lined up surface lines to cement unit and applied 32 bar monitoring
for pressure build-up. Recorded 35 bar pressure build-up to 67 bar. Pressured up to 69 bar and held back pressure.
After 8 hrs, bled off pressure and monitored for pressure build-up. No pressure build-up observed.
Before the casing hanger seal assembly was set, the 14 x 9 5/8” annulus was bullheaded with 82 m3 of 1.10sg NaCl,
corrosion inhibited packer fluid through a closed BOP and the PADPRT at wellhead level.This was done in order to
prevent annulus pressure build up (APB).
A casing scraper assembly was picked up and RIH to 140 m MD and the riser mud was displaced to 1.28sg. Scraped
inside the 9 5/8” casing. Performed USIT logging of the 9 5/8” casing cement. Top of cement fund at 2679 m MD.

4.8 NO 15/9-F-11 B, 8 ½” (06.June.2013 14:30)
START: 06.Jun. 2013 14:30 3197 m MD
END : 16.Jun. 2013 13:30 4770 mMD
OBJECTIVE Drill and geosteer 8 ½” hole to 4859m MD RKB through Hugin reservoir
SUMMARY
The 8 ½” hole was drilled in one bit run from 3197-4770 m MD. Top Hugin encountered at 3468 m MD.
An FIT was conducted to 1.65 sg EMW with 1.28sg mud.While drilling ahead the MW was increased to 1.32 sg as for
the previous 8 ½” sections. No hole problems were seen during this section. At 4624m, the signal from the CoPilot
war lost, and the ASS (steering unit) stopped sending active inclination at 4682m. This caused dropping of the last part
of the well, 100m and it was decided to change TD.
At TD circulated 5 x B/U to ensure a clean wellbore, POOH without any problems or overpull. While pulling out from
3507 to 3453 m the interval was relogged with the MWD/LWD.
BIT RUNS
Run # 1
An 8 ½” Hughes Christensen DP406X type bit was run in hole on 5 ½” HWDP and 5 ½” drill pipe (DP) and drilled
the section in one run.
Parameters: 2000-2500 lpm, 214-238 bar, 1573 m, 80,5 drill hrs, 106,3 circ hrs, 19,5 m/hr, 6-10 tonne, 80-160 rpm, 7 -
30 kNm
Bit grading: 2 – 4 – WT – N – X – I – BT– TD
DRILLING FLUIDS
The rathole was drilled out and an FIT was performed to 1.65 sg with 1.28 sg Enviromul Yellow. When drilling
commenced the mud weight was gradually increased to 1.32sg Enviromul Yellow. At TD, circulated 5 x B/U.
MWD/LWD
A Hughes Christensen DP406X type bit, IDAC M233. A Baker Hughes 6 ¾”AutoTrak steering unit was run to
together with a Baker Hughes MWD and LWD (logging while drilling) for formation evaluation, directional control
and vibration monitoring purposes.
5 ½” heavy weight drill pipe (HWDP) and 5 ½” drill pipe were used above the MWD/LWD string.
LINER
A 7” reamer shoe, three intermediate joints, a float collar and a landing collar was run before 134 joints and 5 pups 7”
29 lb/ft SM13Cr-110 Vam Top was run to setting depth, at 4768 m MD RKB on 5 ½” HWDP and 5 ½” DP landing
string. The liner was pressure tested to 270 bar with the rig pumps.
CEMENTING
Dropped dart # 1 and pumped 26.7 m3 cement. Dropped dart # 2 and displaced cement with rig pumps, all sheared and
bump was observed. Pressure tested the liner to 270 bar with the rig pumps. Sat TSP and shear was observed, pressure
Classification: Internal Status: Final Expiry date: Page 41 of 168

(5227 strokes). No indication of top plug bumped.

REMARKS:
Objective:
Bring TOC to TOL 3099 mtr MD to isolate liner lap. Provide structural support to the liner and zonal isolation across the interval.
Execution:
Mix water was mixed with sea water using CMS pumps that get signals from mix water pump and then give accurate volume of chemicals. This happend in one operation right befor the dry
cement is added.
Spacer pit was cleaned, filled with water and then taken a chloride test. The test shows 350 ppm (maximum 2000 accepted for spacer). Then we added the rest of additives to the spacer and
checked the rheology to be sure the spacer is as supposed.
No losses during the cement job. It was 6 m3 less in return at the end of displacing.
Liner was rotating while cementing.
After 10 min mixing we lost cement transfering from the silo. We then went to our back-up silo and continued as planed. The silos was funktiontested befor the job.
Wiperplug sheared as planned.

1 Well objectives and result
1.1 Well objectives
Objectives of the 15/9-F-11 exploration pilot well were to prove oil in the Volve North prospect and to define the
field OWC. If the pilot would find an oil-down to situation (ODT), an optional openhole side-track further down
flank was planned. In addition the pilot was placed such that it could prove a shallower Hugin interpretation based
on the previous 2009 interpretation, which represented an additional prospect upside. An oil fluid sample was
planned to collect in any commercial find scenario.
Objectives of the second pilot 15/9-F-11 A were to prove oil and sufficient reservoir thickness in the Northwest
fault segment. If the pilot found an oil-water contact (OWC), a water sample would be taken to prove for initial
(perched water) or flooded conditions.
If the 15/9-F-11 North pilot well would encounter a sufficient oil column in Volve North segment, well 15/9-F-11 B
would be drilled to an optimal location further upflank and completed as an oil producer.
If 15/9-F-11 North pilot would find no or insufficient oil in the North prospect, 15/9-F-11 B would be sidetracked to
an infill location between existing producers 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-14 in the main field and downflank towards
existing injector 15/9-F-5. The aim was to geosteer the well in the upper part of the Hugin reservoir, which was
expected to be only partially drained.
Figure 1-1 shows the pre-drill Top Hugin map and the planned well paths.

1.3 Well results
1.3.1 15/9-F-11 / 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect
The well reached on May 4th 2013 at 4561,8 m MD / 3345,2 m MSL its total depth (TD) in the Sleipner Formation.
It encountered water-filled Hugin with some residual oil on logs and cuttings. Top Hugin Formation was
encountered 27 m TVD deeper than expected at 3181 m MSL. Full Hugin stratigraphy was present (104 m TVD /
~98 m TVT thickness). Reservoir properties were slightly poorer than expected, compared to offset wells at similar
depth. Pressure measurements were showing a reservoir pressure 13-14 bar over initial, relative to 15/9-19 BT2
and 15/9-F-15 A water gradient, but in accordance with the main field pressure, which was ~12 bar over initial at
the time. No hydrocarbon indications in Upper Jurassic were observed.
The well proved a dry Volve North prospect. Pre-drill prospect evaluation assumed a P of 34% with main risk
g
elements trap geometry (55%) due to poor seismic control and source migration (70%) given a possible charge
through the Upper Jurassic. The prospect model included a potentially deep OWC contact (down to 3200 m MSL
with no OWC drilled on Volve).
Post-drill well results support a migration fairway through the Hugin Formation into the main field. The prospect
trap however failed due to depth and geometry. Pressure measurements indicate that the aquifer communicates
with the main field. Top Hugin Formation at 3181 m MSL can be considered a water-up to (WUT) observation for
the main field.
A dry North prospect resulted in the cancellation of an immediate 15/9-F-11 B North producer and subsequent
15/9-F-1 A injector option.
1.3.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment
The planned sidetrack reached TD on May 21st 2013 at 3762,0 m MD / 3071,6 m MSL in the Sleipner Formation.
It encountered oil-filled Hugin Formation at 2944 m MSL, thus 25 m TVD deeper than expected. Full Hugin
stratigraphy was present (82 m TVD / ~64 m TVT thickness). Reservoir properties were good, but generally higher
water saturations than in main field oil-bearing wells were observed. Initial pressure measurements were ~12 bar
over initial and in accordance with main field pressure at the time. This indicates pressure communication to the
main field. Time lapse pressure points were taken 48 h later, after injection in main field was closed. This led to 6
bar pressure drop in the main field well 15/9-F-15 C. In the 15/9-F-11 A pilot a ~0.2-0.9 bar pressure drop was
observed, which was consistent with prognosis from the simulation model.
The well proved a thick upside oil column in the NW segment, which is in pressure communication with the main
field. In-place oil volumes in the segment are estimated with ~1.4 MSm3.

Well results triggered planning and drilling of a dedicated injector from slot 15/9-F-1. The injector is supposed to
drive oil towards the existing upflank producer 15/9-F-14 in the main field.
1.3.3 15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field
The planned sidetrack reached TD on June 6th 2013 at 4770,3 m MD / 3203 m MSL in the Sleipner Formation. It
encountered an oil-filled Upper Hugin (zone 3) in heel and toe with good reservoir qualities similar to offset wells.
The total geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long. A total of ~760 m MD in gross oil-
bearing productive Upper Hugin were encountered. Some non-productive Heather Formation and Hugin Thief
Zone (zone 2) intervals were drilled, mainly due to predominantly sub-seismic faulting. Towards the toe, the well
was dropped into water-flooded Thief Zone and Lower Hugin (zone 1) due to steering tool failure. TD was set in
the Sleipner Formation. An exit of the field through the main fault, as expected, was not observed.
With high oil saturation in both toe and heel, the well encountered upside volumes in the targeted Upper Hugin
zone. Expectation was that the heel would be water-filled as indicated by reservoir simulation and 4D seismic,
while mainly the downflank toe section would encounter undrained oil. At ~4700 m MD / 3147 m MSL a marked
drop in resistivity in the Lower Hugin may indicate that the well penetrated the initial field OWC. Water-filled zones
just above showed resistivities, which are consistent with injected Utsira water (+/ produced water).
Initial perforations were placed in good to intermediate quality Upper Hugin sandstones in the toe of well with
optional later perforation in the heel. A total of 93 m were perforated in 8 runs. Production was started July 24th
2013 with an initial nearly water-free rate of ~1000 Sm3/d. Drawdown is limited to avoid early water breakthrough.
Figure 2-1 shows the post-drill Top Hugin Formation depth map with the actual well paths.

2 Geology & Geophysics
2.1 Geological setting and results
2.1.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect
Overburden
Figure 2-1 shows the stratigraphy in the pilot from the Hod Formation down to TD in the Sleipner Formation. Prior
to entering the the Hugin reservoir the well encountered a thick package of Upper Jurassic Draupne and Heather
formations as expected. No significant Intra Draupne sandstones were observed. Thin sandy stringers are mostly
cemented. No indications for hydrocarbons on cuttings or logs were observed.
Figure 2-1 also shows image log interpretation on memory data as delivered by Baker Hughes (reservoir
navigation services, RNS) on memory data. Note that tadpoles are shown undifferentiated/unclassified for type or
confidence level and hence have a high ‘noise level’ (due to picks of cemented intervals etc.). Overall, they are
consistent with the expected structural grain. The well path is transported out in the Shetland Group and dropped
into the downfaulted North prospect while crossing the major fault separating the prospect from the main field.
Tadpoles down to the Draupne Formation indicate a generally North dipping structure with angles of ~10-20°. At
the Heather Formation level structural dip jumps to ~50°, then decreasing with depth into the Hugin Formation to
~30°. This indicates a structural drag along the large fault to the South. Formation is dipping generally to the NNE
to NE which is approximately consistent with the formation dip on the Top Hugin Formation depth map.
Reservoir section
A complete Hugin stratigraphy was encountered in the well. Lithostratigraphic subzonation can easily be
correlated with offset wells indicating no significant lateral facies changes.
Figure 2-2 shows the reservoir section on logs with the Hugin subzonation in track 3.

to Chapter 6 for full petrophysical evaluation and CPI.
Pre-drill vs post-drill segment evaluation
Oil shows in the well are documented in Chapter 4. Residual oil intervals on logs are documented in Chapter 6.
The presence of some hydrocarbons in the Hugin Formation supports a migration through the Hugin Formation
from the kitchen in the North into the main field via the 15/9-F-11 T2 location. An additional fairway in Intra
Draupne sandstones has not been proven.
Pre-drill prospect evaluation assumed a P of 34% with main risk elements trap geometry (55%) due to poor
g
seismic control and source migration (70%) given a possible charge through the Upper Jurassic. The prospect
model included a potentially deep OWC contact (down to 3200 m MSL; no OWC drilled on Volve). With top
reservoir coming in deeper, the area is below the Volve Field closure and was hence bypassed (Figure 1-2).
Pressure points indicate that the drilled aquifer communicates with the main field (Chapter 3.5.1). Top Hugin
Formation at 3181 m MSL can be considered a WUT observation for the main field.

2.1.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment
Reservoir section
A complete Hugin stratigraphy was encountered in the well. Lithostratigraphic subzonation can easily be
correlated with offset wells indicating no significant lateral facies changes.
Fig 2.3 shows the reservoir section on logs with the Hugin subzonation in track 3.
• Upper Hugin (zones 3.3-3.1): 3594.6-3612.5 m MD
With ~3 m TVD thick offshore shale at base
o
• Middle Hugin or ‘Thief Zone’ (zones 2.3-2.1): 3612.5-3653 m MD
with typical high permeability upper and lower zone as confirmed by high mobilities
o
• Lower Hugin (zones 1.6-1.1): 3653-3684 m MD
The thickness of the Hugin Formation was with 82 m TVD / ~64 m TVT near twice the expected. No direct
indications for faults on RT image logs are observed (Figure 2-3; image log tadpoles track 11). Note that
downfaulting would lead to an apparent thickening of the Hugin unit.
The reservoir section is overall very sandy as indicated by a N/G of 86%. Average porosity of 20% is comparable
to offset wells at similar depth. Reference to Chapter 6 for full petrophysical evaluation and CPI. Main differences
to offset wells in the main field are the higher water saturations (Sw) observed consistently throughout the entire
Hugin section. Average Sw is 32%, and hence >10% higher than in main field. Based on the fact that these high
saturations are observed throughout the Hugin reservoir and not bound to individual zones, they are not
considered drainage effects, and overall difficult to explain.
Pre-drill vs post-drill segment evaluation
Prior to drilling, the segment was risked with a P of 63%. The main risk elements were reservoir presence (70%)
g
due to potential erosion and source migration (90%) given a charge through Hugin via the main field and a
possible disconnect due to faulting.
The well proved a thick upside oil column in the NW segment. Pressure points show full communication with the
main field (Chapter 3.5.2). In-place oil volumes in the segment are estimated with ~1.4 MSm3. The main
uncertainties for in-place volumes are thickness of Hugin further upflank from the 15/9-F-11 A location and the
location of the major segment bounding faults especially also towards the salt collapse area in the South.

2.1.3 15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field
Reservoir section
Figure 2-4 shows the reservoir section with the reservoir zonation in track 4. Table 2-1 provides the zonation as
list. Geosteering experiences are summarised in Chapter 2.3.4. Reservoir zonation is based on the curtain model
provided by Baker Hughes’ realtime reservoir navigation service (RNS) using all available log information,
specifically azimuthal resistivity readings (by AziTrak) and real-time image log interpretation from azimuthal
gamma ray (GR) and azimuthal density (by LithoTrak). Data is compared to a pre-drill model and offset wells
(mainly well 15/9-F-5), and re-matched in real-time during drilling in cooperation with PETEK G&G.
Zonation documented in this report deviates from the final Baker Hughes delivery in some small intervals. Overall
confidence is high, due to the Heather Formation and Thief Zone ‘calibration points’.
The well encountered an oil-bearing Upper Hugin in heel and toe with good reservoir qualities similar to offset
wells. The total geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long. A total ~760 m MD in gross oil-
bearing productive Upper Hugin (zones 3.1-3.3) was encountered.
Non-productive Heather intervals were mainly due to predominantly sub-seismic faulting. Thief Zone intervals
were water-flooded as expected. A short upflank interval of water flooded Upper Hugin at 3528-3539 m MD (zone
3.2) was not repeated further down in the well.
Towards the toe, the well was dropped into water-flooded Thief Zone and Lower Hugin due to tool failure. TD was
set in the Sleipner Formation. An exit of the field through the main fault, as expected, was not observed.
With high oil saturation in both toe and heel, the well encountered upside volumes in the targeted Upper Hugin
zone. Pre-drill expectation was that the heel would be water filled as indicated by reservoir simulation and 4D
seismic, while mainly the downflank toe section would encounter undrained oil.
At ~ 4700 m MD / 3147 m MSL a marked drop in resistivity to <0.7 Ohm.m in the Lower Hugin may indicate that
the well penetrated the initial field OWC. Water-flooded zones just above show resistivities of approximately 1
Ohm.m, which are consistent with injected Utsira water (+/ produced water).

2.2 Seismic interpretation versus well results
Sonic logs (both compressional and shear) were acquired in the two pilot wells 15/9-F-11 T2 and 15/9-F-11 A.
Checkshots and VSP were acquired in 15/9-F-11 T2.
2.2.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect
Top and Base Hugin Formation depth prognosis in the North prospect were initially very uncertain. The seismic
reflectivity in this area is very poor in all datasets and together with the presence of an undrilled thick Upper
Jurassic package in this downfaulted segment the uncertainties on Top and Base Hugin Formation prognoses
were set to ±70 m TVD. Top Hugin Formation came in +29 m TVD deeper and Base Hugin Formation came in
+33 m TVD deeper than prognosis.
Checkshot pairs and VSP (normal incident and rig source) were acquired. The synthetic seismic from well logs
confirmed a poor seismic signal on Top Hugin Formation and a weak seismic signal on Base Hugin Formation
compared to Base Hugin Formation on the main field (Figure 2-5). Using checkshots as time depth curve a
deeper time interpretation of Top and Base Hugin Formation is initiated. This information has been implemented
in the updated maps. VSP image data is processed by Schlumberger and preliminary results indicate a more
northern placement of the large main bounding fault compared to initial seismic interpretation. These preliminary
observations have also been implemented in the updated maps. It was not observed any improvement of Top and
Base Hugin reflectors on the preliminary results. Final imaging results of VSP processing are estimated to be
delivered during November 2013.
Classification: Internal Status: Final Expiry date: 2010-12-30 Page 23 of 110

observations have also been implemented in the updated maps. It was not observed any improvement of Top and Base Hugin reflectors on the preliminary results. 2.2.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment
Seismic reflectivity is in general poor also in the Northwest segment. In some parts it is assumed to be a good
seismic signal on Base Hugin Formation, but it varies across the area, possibly due to faulting. Top Hugin
Formation interpretation is model driven due to Hugin Formation probably within tuning of BCU. In the initial
seismic interpretation both Top and Base Hugin Formation had uncertainties of -15/+55 m TVD.
Top Hugin came in +24 m TVD deeper and Base Hugin came in +48 m TVD deeper than prognosed. From
synthetic seismic it can be observed that Top Hugin Formation is within tuning of BCU (Top Draupne Formation)
and that Base Hugin Formation is a good seismic event and as expected from seismic modelling and
observations on the main field. The deeper observations of Top and Base Hugin Formation and the observation of
a thicker Hugin package lead to an update of Top and Base Hugin Formation maps in the Northwest segment.
Synthetic seismic and updated interpretations are shown in Figure 2-6.

2.2.3 15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field
In the F-11 B IOR producer Top Hugin Formation came in -7 m TVD, i.e shallower than prognosed depth
(uncertainty on depth estimate was ±20 m TVD). Sonic logging was neither planned nor performed in this high
deviated well. No major surprises were encountered during drilling of the reservoir section and the smaller faults
on the Volve main structure were targeted within the uncertainty span given upfront drilling. The well was planned
to drill through the main bounding fault to the East, but this fault was not reached on the prognosed location and
the TD of well were set in the Sleipner Formation. Based on this, the seismic interpretation was updated at the toe
of the well and the main bounding fault of the Volve Field is moved further East in the updated maps of Top and
Base Hugin Formation (Figure 2-7).

26” section
When continue drilling from 224 m ROP was held back to max 10 m/h to keep vertical direction. At 273 m the
OnTrak inclination showed increasing inclination 0.71º. Gyro surveys on wireline approved that inclination.
Calculations in the Compass software show a technical collision when projecting to the bit (40 m). While drilling
ahead to 347 m extensive reaming took place to get back to vertical. It was not possible to turn the well around
into the opposite previous hole direction, instead it drifted closer to the two already drilled wells beneath a
separation factor of 1. The inclination ranges in this section by 0.2°-0.7°, 0.7° (start-end, max).

2.3.2 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect
26” section
The inclination ranges in this section by 0.0°-2.5°, 2.5° (start-end, max).
The Utsira Formation was entered at 887 m, where the inclination was build up to 24º. Rotating in order to
observe behaviour resulted in a 2º drop. The remaining of the Utsira sandstones was drilled in orienting mode in
an attempt to hold or build the inclination to 22º. Toolface control in Utsira Formation was ultimate difficult due to
intermittent hardness of the formation was encountered, resulting in an uncontrollable deviation. Entering the
Skade Formation the steering deteriorated even further. Very soft formations were encountered, making it
impossible to rotate through it (losing angle). Steering required a lot of effort and time.

17 ½” section
The inclination ranges in this section by 22.9°-36.3°, 36.3° (start-end, max).
The deviation was 22.9 º inclination and 278º azimuth at the start. The well trajectory was very far from the plan
due to having been unable in the previous 26'' section to build and turn in unconsolidated sandstones of the Utsira
and the Skade formations with a motor. To move progressively towards the original plan involved building
inclination from 24º-35.8° and turning left from 283º-253° azimuth by 1592 m at a rate of 2.5°/30 m. Then a
tangent section was drilled to 1699 m, followed by a drop and turn with 2.5°/30 m to 2128 m. Inclination was
dropped down to 4.6° and the well turned right to 339° azimuth. Finally, a continuous build and turn with 2.5°/30 m
was performed to TD at 2580 m where the inclination is 37° and the azimuth 69.6°. At TD the 17 1/2" section was
well placed with 1.36 m below and 0.46 m left of the planned well path.
8 ½” section
This almost 2000 m MD long section started with an inclination of 36.1º, built up to 82.0º over the first 450 m in the
Shetland Group, held tangent over 700 m and dropped back to 14.4º above reservoir. No turn was planned, thus
the well continued on the same 69.9º azimuth and 14.4º inclination for a further 200 m to TD. The initial build in
the Lista and Ty formations went without problems, the tool gave more than the theoretical calculated rate. This
changed dramatically in the Tor limestone, where it became extremely difficult to achieve the required 3.0º/30 m
build rate. With the AutoTrak tool in steer mode with 100% steer force, it was still necessary to cut back on the
flow rate and hold back ROP to 5 m/h to get it to build. Consequently, over approximately 160 m, the well path
dropped some 14 m below the line. This was recovered in the tangent section by increasing the sail angle from
80.2 to 82.0º. Stick/slip values increased in the Shetland Group. The drop from 80.0° to 14.0º inclination was
started from 3710 m. Initially the tool response was as expected, but in the lower Hod Formation and into the
Cromer Knoll Group the drop rate showed a large increase. Drilling continued to section TD without further
problems.
The section and plan views for the wellbore (from slot to TD at 4562 m MD) are shown in Figure 2-10.

2.3.3 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment
8 ½” section
Start inclination for the section was 37.6°, build to approximately 75° turning left and drop again to 39.9° at TD.
The sidetrack off the cement plug went without any issues. The planned wellpath could easily be followed after
that, with the AutoTrak capable of 7º/30 m. The drilling through the Tor Formation proved not to be that difficult
this time (compared with 15/9-F-11 T2). The tangent inclination was therefore reduced to 75º, to get closer to the
line again prior to start the drop interval at 3187 m. From around 3302 m (in the lower Hod Formation) onwards,
highly fluctuating near-bit inclination readings were seen. The drop rate became too high, and consequently the
drop force had to be reduced considerably to limit the drop in inclination. After passing this trouble zone
approximately 3400 m, drilling continued without any further directional problems. In the Tor and Hod limestones
the drilling parameters had to be varied regularly to limit the stick/slip levels.

8 ½” section (geosteering section)
Objectives
The IOR well 15/9-F-11 B was planned to be placed in the Upper Hugin zone to drain remaining reserves
between the production wells 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-14 and continuing downflank towards water injector 15/9-F-5.
The planned well path was already characterised by challenging steering, thus beside the main focus on
horizontal steering, also sufficient turn in azimuth to prolong the reservoir section as deep into the structure as
possible needed to be ensured.
Summary
Against interpretation from 4D seismic analysis and reservoir simulation the target zone within Upper Hugin
showed good oil saturation throughout the reservoir section. Estimates of true vertical thicknesses (TVT) indicate
thicker reservoir upflank than expected. TD was set at 4770 m MD, as the wellbore entered Sleipner Formation
below Hugin Formation, without penetrating the anticipated southwesterly field-bounding fault. The total
geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long. A total ~760 m MD in gross oil-bearing
productive Upper Hugin (zones 3.1-3.3) was encountered.
Geosteering
The pre-model from Baker Hughes (RNS) indicated higher contrast towards top reservoir, i.e. Heather Formation
and very low contrasts towards Thief Zone below. Additionally are several faults along the well path prognosed,
causing down-faulted reservoirs
For dip calculations the azimuthal GR (11 m behind bit) and azimuthal DEN (24 m behind bit) were used, also to
some extent distance to bed (D2B) calculations. Hence geosteering commands were affected by often significant
time delays. Several faults were crossed, causing 3 times an unexpected/non-pre-warned exit of top reservoir
(D2B ranged from 3,5-4,1 m TVD). Entering of the reservoir again was just estimated based on the geological
model and steering with maximum dogleg downwards. According to Baker Hughes’ RNS top reservoir cannot be
detected earlier than 1-2 m TVD from above. D2B calculations were not always applicable for bed boundary
detection, due to complex resistivity profile. Another 3 times the well continued from Upper Hugin into the
underlying Thief Zone (yellow circle). Here no distinct signal response was observed. Distances to bed were
roughly estimated to range from 1,5-2,0 m TVD.
Geosteering decisions were made by the Volve PETEK with support from GEOP and the Baker Hughes’ RNS
team. The RNS supervisor was present in the Statoil office in Bergen during office hours, while RNS support
outside office hours was covered by the Baker Hughes’ BEACON center in Stavanger.
Wellpath description
28 real-time geosteering decisions were performed. Any changes in trajectory was done to optimise wellbore
positioning in the reservoir and to avoid shaly and water-filled zones respectively.
- Started drilling reservoir section from 3198,0 m MD / 2783,8 m TVD. Increased inclination from 63° to 71°.
- Entered Heather Formation at 3351,6 m MD / 2846,6 m TVD. Possible fault with minor throw at 3414 m
MD, indicated by high angle dip. Possibly repetition of penetrated stratigraphy.

angle features indicates faulting at entry and exit of Heather Formation. Estimated throws are ~0.5 m.
- Hugin 3.3 thickness >9 m TVD. Drilled down through Hugin 3, penetrated base Hugin 3 twice (due to

~3 m. Building angle to enter lowermost part of reservoir before crossing major prognosed fault F3.
- Entered base of reservoir at 4188,0 m MD by faulting (F3), estimated throw 11 m. Continuously dropping
angle to align with formation dip and land wellbore in Hugin 3.3. Stopped planned left turn of well path to
maximise drop force. Minor faulting while drilling upwards in Hugin 3.
- Exited reservoir through fault (F4) with estimated throw of 18 m and 24° angle between wellbore and fault.

planned left turn of well path to maximise drop force and enter reservoir before TD.

dropping angle to penetrate complete reservoir interval and assess reservoir thickness. Possible minor fault
around 4515 m MD (F5?). Exited Hugin 3 at 4573,0 m MD / 3113.9 m TVD.
- Holding angle of 42° after Hugin 2.2. No active steering possible after 4685 m MD, due to AutoTrak failure.

3.4 Electrical logging, VSP
Planning
In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 to Volve North prospect a vertical seismic profiling survey (VSP) was planned. Purpose
of the survey was:
- primary: more accurate time-depth relationship,
- secondary objective: improve imaging of reservoir and faults.
The feasibility study and pre-job modelling was performed by Thor Allan Garden (thgar@statoil.com, VSP group).
Based on previous results from 15/9-F-15 A and recent seismic data a walk-away survey with 2 sources was
recommended.
Schlumberger’s VSI-4 had been selected and after evaluation of operational risk, especially the highly deviated
well path (maximum 82° in open hole), finally a pump down VSP inside a simple BHA was selected.
Operations
A rig source VSP and a vertical incidence VSP (flip-flop configuration) were recorded in the 8 ½” open hole, the
14” and the 20” cased hole. The sources were 3x250 cu.in G-guns, and a Trisor OFS used for advanced gun
performance control.
Vertical incidence survey interval was recorded between 4530.0- 2228.8 m MD, the rig source survey 4530-582,9
m at 15 m intervals.
While RIH checkshots were taken at 1786,6 and 2518,4 m MD; a correlation pass was run from 4450 m up. While
POOH and measuring communication interference between rig and the vessel Dina Star were experienced at
2228,8 m. The survey was continued with the rig source only.
Results
Preliminary VSP imaging results indicated a more northern placement (~30-50 m) of the main bounding fault to
the north compared to the initial seismic interpretation. Final processing is pending.
Classification: Internal Status: Final Expiry date: 2010-12-30 Page 59 of 110

3.5 Pressure points
3.5.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect
In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 at 6 depths pressures points in the water-filled Hugin reservoir were taken (Table 3-5).
This was performed while POOH after TD on May 04th and 5th 2013. At this time both water injectors (15/9-F-4
and 15/9-F-5) were in operation. 6 of 6 tests were successful and of good technical quality (all reached a stable
pressure).
Pressure points were showing a reservoir pressure 13-14 bar over initial, relative to 15/9-19 BT2 and 15/9-F-15 A
water gradient, but in accordance with the main field pressure, which was ~12 bar over initial at the time (Figure
3-1).

In pilot well 15/9-F-11 A all in all 24 pressure stations in the oil-bearing Hugin Formation were taken. 17 (+2
repeated) were performed while POOH after TD on May 21st 2013 (Table 3-6). At this time both water injectors
(15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5) were in operation. On May 23th 2013 additional 5 pressure measurements were repeated.
This time after both water injectors were shutdown over a period of almost 48 hours. 18 of 24 tests (2 sets of data
with time delay) were successful and of good technical quality (one might be supercharged). 6 tests were tight.
Formation pressure points in the Hugin Formation were 12 bar above initial formation pressure in oil zone
(measured in 15/9-F-12, February 2008) (Figure 3-1). This indicates communication with the Hugin Formation in
the Volve main field, since reservoir pressure has been increased ~12 bar above initial pressure by injection at
the time.
Time lapse repeated pressure points (Figure 3-1) after closing in main field injections were 0.2-0.9 bar lower than
48hrs earlier. At the same time a pressure drop of 6 bar was recorded in the main field pressure gauge in well
15/9-F-15 C. The small pressure drop was consistent with prognosis from the simulation model.

3.6 Reservoir fluid sampling
Objectives
In both pilot wells optional fluid sampling was planned.
- 15/9-F-11 (T2): 1-2 oil samples for compositional analysis, density and viscosity & 1 water sample for
resistivity measurements as water saturation reference for the petrophysical model as well compositional
analysis, density, viscosity were planned to take, if the Hugin Formation in North prospect is oil filled or an
oil-water contact is been encountered.
- 15/9-F-11 A: 1 water sample for compositional analysis, density and viscosity to define water type (initial
formation water or perched water), if the Hugin Formation in Northwest segment is partly oil-bearing.
Since the open hole wellpaths of both 8 ½” pilot sections were planned with inclinations of 82° in 15/9-F-11 (T2)
and 75° in 15/9-F-11 A respectively a feasibility study by Schlumberger recommended pipe conveyed wireline
logging for running MDT.
Summary
In none of the pilot wells the criteria were achieved. Well 15/9-F-11 T2 found water-filled the Hugin Formation in
the North prospect/segment, whereas well 15/9-F-11 A proved the entire Hugin reservoir oil-bearing in the
Northwest segment. Therefore all MDT runs were cancelled.

5 Pore pressures and hole stability
5.1 Formation pressure above the reservoir
The prediction of the pore pressure gradient (PP) above the reservoir is based on the Eaton Sonic method.
- A hydrostatic pore pressure gradient is expected down to top Ty Formation. The pore pressure within
permeable Ty sandstones was measured in wells 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-15 S as 0.87 sg. This indicates
some 40 bar pressure depletion caused by production from the Sleipner Øst Field in the South. Recent
pressure data from Sleipner well 15/9-A-24, the last gas condensate producer, show stable pressure since
2005, in line with the stop of production in the northern Sleipner Øst area. The pore pressure in the Ty
Formation within the Volve area is expected to be 0.87 sg, as measured in 2007.
- In the underlying upper part of the Shetland Group the pore pressure is assumed to increase gently up to
1.05 sg within the Hod Formation. For the rest of the Shetland and the Cromer Knoll Group a rapid increase
in the pore pressure to 1.26 sg is prognosed.
- The maximum pore pressure within the Viking Group is expected to be 1.29 sg in the organic rich Draupne
shales at the top. The pore pressure within the underlying Heather Formation is expected to be lower.
5.2 Pore pressure in the reservoir
The reservoir pressure in the Hugin Formation main field is currently maintained at approximately 10-12 bar over
initial pressure by injecting water downflank in wells 15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5. Throughout the field an oil gradient of
0,0704 bar/m and a water gradient of 0.103 bar/m is assumed. Pressure measurements were undertaken in both
pilot wells (Chapter 3.5).
- In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 pressure range was measured to be 13-14 bar over initial in the water zone.
- Pilot well 15/9-F-11 A proved a pressure of 12 bar above initial formation pressure in oil zone.
- For IOR well 15/9-F-11 B the pressure in the Hugin Formation has been estimated to be 12 bar over initial
(June 2013), referring to time equivalent pressure measurements at downhole pressure gauges in Volve
main field.
At the base of the Heather Formation likely bad to moderate sandstones in the North prospect and Northwest
segment are expected to be present. Even there is not often interpreted flow potential at all; this zone has been
anticipated to correspond to reservoir pressure.

A minimum horizontal strength gradient has been calculated from a Breckels and van Eekelen relation based
on a method from Per Horsrud (2011). Initial reservoir pressure is used.
- Formation strength in the Balder Formation is reduced due to the presence of tuff layers. Nevertheless
XLOT data from well 15/9-F-14 showed a minimum horizontal stress gradient (ShG) of 1.68 sg in the upper
part of the Balder Formation. In the lower 32 m of the Balder Formation the formation strength is reduced to
1.55 sg to account for local experiences.
- Within the Ty reservoir the formation strength is assumed to be reduced due to depletion, but should not
cause any hole stability problems. It is well above the anticipated pore pressure in the overburden section.
- The Minifrac data from the Hugin Formation in well 15/9-19 A was revaluated. The result is a 19 bar
reduction in the minimum horizontal stress gradient for the Hugin reservoir and the formations below.
The fracture gradient for the Volve area is set as 5% above the minimum horizontal strength, according to the
model.
- In the upper part of the Balder Formation an adjustment to only 1.73 sg was done to account for reduced
formation strength due to the presence of tuff layers.
A collapse gradient has been generated based on the method of Stassi d’Alia. Included in this method are the
initial pore pressure, the minimum horizontal stress, considerations of the UCS (unconfined compressive
strength), and the well path.
- The collapse curve in the Nordland Group and upper Hordaland Group shows a general increase towards
the hydrostatic pore pressure and surpasses it in the Skade Formation. The 36” & 26” sections were drilled
with 1.03 sg seawater/spud mud successfully.
- The Hordaland shales are normally associated with high smectite content and under-compaction, causing
instability and possibly higher pore pressure in the lower Hordaland Group. However, in the Volve area the
sandstones of the Grid Formation are generally present, acting as a pressure vent and thereby preventing
abnormal pore pressure. Nevertheless, shales within the Hordaland Group have not been observed as
unstable, when drilled at high inclinations and particularly when exposed to water based drilling fluid.
The UCS has been updated/increased by a factor of 1.2 while planning the 2nd drilling campaign
o
and re-evaluating earlier results. Positive experiences from well 15/9-F-5, where the lower
Hordaland and the Rogaland Group have been drilled with 1.35 sg OBM at inclinations of 27°-31°
without any incidents and on the other hand tight hole conditions in well 15/9-F-12 with an
inclination <10° also with 1.35 sg OBM were taken into account.
A maximum collapse gradient of 1.34 sg is estimated (maximum 23° inclination). No incidents have been
observed when drilling with 1.40 sg standard OBM.
- A peak in the collapse gradient is calculated at the base of the Lista Formation. There is no definite
evidence for significant unstable conditions in this interval, but washouts were observed on MWD logs and
sticky problems occurred when running casing on some previous wells. However post-drilling the UCS was
adjusted/increased by a factor 1.15 to account for no incidents when drilling this lower part with 1.28 sg
standard OBM, and several kick-off from the here placed casing shoe.
- No incidents have been observed in the Cromer Knoll Group.

6 Petrophysical evaluation
6.1 Summary
6.1.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect
The Hugin Formation was water-filled, except of two pockets of residual oil that seem to be structurally trapped.
(Lowest Sw achieved in these is ~ 0.4) One residual oil pocket is situated below the Heather Formation and the
other below the “offshore shale” which separates Upper Hugin from the “Thief Zone” (Hugin zone 2).
These 2 residual oil pockets are observed in the following intervals:
4393.8-4399 m MD / 3182.3-3187.3 m MSL,
4415.8-4419.7 m MD / 3203.6-3207.4 m MSL.
An oil show is described on cuttings @ 4411 m MD.
The rest of the Hugin Formation, which was water-filled, showed very low resistivities < 0.05 Ohm.m in the best
sands, is reflecting the very saline formation water on Volve (~ 130000 ppm). This might indicate that the Hugin
Formation never has been oil-filled, and that the two observed oil pockets are results of hydrocarbon migration.
The Hugin Formation properties seem to be poorer than encountered in previous Volve wells, with an average
porosity of 0.17. This is 4 p.u. less than the porosity in the Hugin Formation in the exploration well 15/9-19 BT2,
which is the closest well with respect to TVD depth. Net/Gross is however high as expected (Hugin > 0.8); in this
well 0.81.
Porosity from MagTrak is a lithology independent porosity, and MagTrak porosity in 15/9-F-11 T2 confirms in
general the porosity from the density log.
Formation pressure points in the Hugin Formation were 13.7 bar above initial formation pressure measured in
15/9-19 BT2 (January 1998). This indicates communication with the Hugin Formation in the Volve main field,
since reservoir pressure has been increased ~12 bar above initial pressure by injection, prior to this drilling
campaign.
LWD log data is of good quality.
No oil and water samples were taken since no commercial find.
